title: Aggregations Recognition Track — Senior
description: A 15-exercise senior recognition track. Read production-scale pipeline snippets, physical plans, per-task metrics, multi-day incident timelines, and log excerpts — answer about what is happening, why, and what would change at the architectural level. Five modules: JAS1 Catalyst & AQE · JAS2 Bucketed & Pre-Aggregated · JAS3 Multi-Hop Joins · JAS4 Streaming Exactly-Once · JAS5 Cross-Team Postmortems. Companion to PSS2/4/5/6/7/9/10.

datasets.description: Reference meter readings (20 rows). Senior scenarios reference much larger production tables (3.5M smart meters, 8 TB archive, multi-country); the CSV is a schema-level reference, not the production data.
Substations reference (6 rows). Schema-only; production scale is much larger.
Per-customer-type tariffs (4 rows). Schema-only; production has historical tariff versions per country.

tasks.title: Stale Statistics Defeat Broadcast

description.context: GridUnion Continental, German subsidiary. The DE Analytics team owns a nightly aggregation that joins meter readings to a customer dimension and produces a Gold daily-customer-summary table consumed by the Billing team's hourly job. The pipeline has been running cleanly for eleven months. This week it began running 7× slower (8 minutes → 58 minutes) with no code change. The dimension-side table is reported in the plan as 8MB, well below the 10MB autoBroadcastJoinThreshold, but the plan shows a SortMergeJoin rather than the BroadcastHashJoin that ran for the previous eleven months.
description.task: Read the physical plan and the per-task metrics, then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true
+- HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#42, region#11, date#15], functions=[sum(daily_kw#16), avg(price_eur_per_kwh#88), count(1)])
   +- AQEShuffleRead coalesced
      +- ShuffleQueryStage 4
         +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_type#42, region#11, date#15, 200)
            +- HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#42, region#11, date#15], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#16), partial_avg(price_eur_per_kwh#88), partial_count(1)])
               +- Project [customer_type#42, region#11, date#15, daily_kw#16, price_eur_per_kwh#88]
                  +- SortMergeJoin [customer_id#14], [customer_id#180], Inner
                     :- Sort [customer_id#14 ASC NULLS FIRST], false, 0
                     :  +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id#14, 200)
                     :     +- FileScan parquet silver_de.meters_enriched [customer_id#14, region#11, daily_kw#16, date#15, customer_type#42, price_eur_per_kwh#88]
                     +- Sort [customer_id#180 ASC NULLS FIRST], false, 0
                        +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id#180, 200)
                           +- FileScan parquet silver_de.customers [customer_id#180]
                              Statistics: sizeInBytes=8.0 MiB

evidence.title: Stage 3 — Exchange hashpartitioning on the customer-side scan (build side of the join)

fields.label: Q1 — The plan reports the build side as 8MB but task 3.0 actually read 4.1GB. What does this size mismatch indicate about the source of the size estimate?
options: Spark's runtime size detection sampled a single Parquet row group rather than aggregating file footers, and the sampled group was atypically small at 8MB while the full table is 4.1GB.
The 8MB is a persisted catalog statistic from a previous ANALYZE TABLE; the table grew to 4.1GB without any later refresh of the statistic.
Catalyst inferred 8MB from the column-pruned projection footer estimate, which collapses dictionary-encoded columns to a fraction of their true scan-time size.
AQE's runtime size injection overwrote the planning-time estimate to 8MB during plan conversion, and the underlying scan never produced an authoritative size at all.
fields.correctAnswer: The 8MB is a persisted catalog statistic from a previous ANALYZE TABLE; the table grew to 4.1GB without any later refresh of the statistic.
fields.rationale:
The 8MB figure visible in the plan as 'Statistics: sizeInBytes=8.0 MiB' is the table's persisted statistic from the Hive metastore (or the equivalent Unity Catalog statistic). Catalyst reads this statistic at planning time to decide join strategy. If ANALYZE TABLE has not been run since the table grew, the statistic reflects an old size — in this case the size from when the table was first created. Catalyst has no native mechanism to detect stale statistics; it trusts the catalog. The actual 4.1GB is what the file scan reads at runtime, but by then the join strategy has already been chosen.

When this answer is wrong: On Spark 3.5+ with the cost-based optimizer enabled and statistics auto-refresh configured at the catalog level, the 8MB figure could legitimately be a runtime-sampled estimate. In that case the right answer would lean toward C. GridUnion's catalog does not have auto-refresh enabled — the platform team has been deferring that change for two quarters.

fields.label: Q2 — Given the stale 8MB statistic, why did AQE not convert the SortMergeJoin into a BroadcastHashJoin once the actual size became visible at runtime?
options: AQE conversion runs on the materialized output of the build-side shuffle stage; once that stage produced 3.8GB, the build side was no longer broadcast-eligible and conversion correctly declined.

AQE conversion is skipped when the join was a BroadcastHashJoin candidate at planning time; runtime conversion only operates on plans that started life as a SortMergeJoin candidate that fell below threshold.

AQE conversion fired and produced a converted plan, but the resulting broadcast exceeded the driver's available memory budget so the converted plan was rolled back to the original SortMergeJoin shape.
AQE conversion is disabled by default in Spark 3.5 unless spark.sql.adaptive.localShuffleReader.enabled has been explicitly set to true on the cluster level for this workload.
fields.correctAnswer: AQE conversion runs on the materialized output of the build-side shuffle stage; once that stage produced 3.8GB, the build side was no longer broadcast-eligible and conversion correctly declined.
fields.rationale:
AQE's runtime join-strategy conversion (sometimes called dynamic join selection) operates on the actual materialized size of the build side after its preceding shuffle stage completes. Here, once the shuffle output of stage 3 was materialized at 3.8GB, AQE correctly observed that the build side was no longer broadcast-eligible — well above the 10MB threshold — and therefore did not convert. AQE's behavior here is correct given the runtime evidence. The architectural failure is upstream, in the planning-time decision that picked SortMergeJoin in the first place when broadcast would have been correct.

When this answer is wrong: In Spark 3.4 and earlier, AQE conversion had stricter preconditions (only triggered on the leftmost shuffle stage of a join), and the 'never converts back to broadcast once the SMJ shuffle has run' framing of option A is too strong. In Spark 3.5+ this is the dominant behavior.

fields.label: Q3 — Identify the architectural commitment that has failed here. The remedy is at the platform level, not at the pipeline level — name the platform-level remedy.
options: Commitment: AQE was assumed to rescue every stale-statistics join at runtime. Remedy: enable spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled cluster-wide, which generalizes runtime adaptation to all asymmetric joins not just skewed ones.

Commitment: at deployment the table fit the broadcast threshold and the engineer assumed it always would. Remedy: a platform-level scheduled ANALYZE TABLE for every Silver table touched by joins on a known cadence.

Commitment: the cluster was sized for a SortMergeJoin workload and is under-provisioned for the actual data sizes. Remedy: scale the cluster horizontally to accommodate the data volume that nightly aggregations now process.

Commitment: the join key was chosen for cardinality rather than data locality during initial design. Remedy: re-bucket both sides on customer_id so the bucketed-join optimization fires and avoids the shuffle entirely.
fields.correctAnswer: Commitment: at deployment the table fit the broadcast threshold and the engineer assumed it always would. Remedy: a platform-level scheduled ANALYZE TABLE for every Silver table touched by joins on a known cadence.
fields.rationale:
The architectural commitment is the implicit assumption that the table size at first deployment is the table size forever. Catalogs do not refresh statistics automatically in vanilla Spark; ANALYZE TABLE is a manual operation that the platform team or the table owner must schedule. The remedy is platform-level because every table touched by joins has the same exposure — a per-pipeline fix would scale linearly with pipeline count and fail by attrition. The standard pattern is a platform-owned scheduled job that runs ANALYZE TABLE on every Silver table whose row count has changed by more than N% since the last analysis, paired with monitoring that alerts when a table has gone unanalyzed for longer than the configured cadence.

When this answer is wrong: If the cluster is genuinely under-provisioned for the data volume (option C is sometimes the right diagnosis), or if the join key is the actual root cause and the size mismatch is incidental, a different remedy applies. The diagnostic tell that B is the right answer here is the size jump from 8MB stale to 4.1GB actual — an order-of-magnitude mismatch, not a sizing problem.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level monitoring rule that catches this class of incident is a 'statistics freshness' dashboard that alerts on any Silver table whose last_analyzed timestamp is older than the configured cadence (typically 7 days for tables larger than 1GB). GridUnion adopted this rule after a similar incident in 2025 Q3 and has not seen a stale-statistics broadcast regression since.

Deferred short answer (Q4): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The Wednesday-night runtime jumped from 8 minutes to 58 minutes between two consecutive nightly runs. What event most likely happened in the eleven-month interval that finally produced the regression on this specific Wednesday?
→ The customer table grew steadily across the eleven months — likely incremental ingestion from the four-country acquisition — and on this Wednesday it crossed the 10MB autoBroadcastJoinThreshold. Catalyst's planning-time statistic was still 8MB (no ANALYZE TABLE had run since deployment), so it picked SortMergeJoin once the actual size exceeded what the broadcast strategy could tolerate; for the previous eleven months, even at the stale 8MB statistic, the resulting BroadcastHashJoin happened to still work because the actual size was just under the threshold. The crossing point is when the architectural defect surfaces.

tasks.pssReference: PSS2 lesson 2 (Statistics Propagation), PSS2 lesson 3 (CBO Cost Model), PSS2 lesson 4 (AQE Runtime Adaptation Mechanics); also references mid-level JAI2.4 (broadcast hint OOM — same threshold, opposite direction).

tasks.title: Custom Catalyst Rule Reorders the Plan

description.context: GridUnion Continental, cross-country aggregation pipeline owned by the Platform team. The pipeline reads from a Platinum view that joins meters, customers, and tariffs across all four countries (DE, FR, NL, LU) and computes per-country compliance summaries for the BNetzA, CRE, ACM, and ILR audit reports. A new engineer on the Platform team reviews the plan for a DE-only query and notices that the country='DE' filter appears earlier in the plan than the source code suggests it should — it is pushed down past a join that the code clearly defines after the filter. The engineer suspects a bug; the team lead suspects something else.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — the team_context block names the operational pressures in play — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, count

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("de_compliance_summary").getOrCreate()

# Platinum view: cross-country meters, customers, tariffs
platinum = spark.read.table("platinum.cross_country.meter_customer_tariff")

# Regulatory classifications: ~200 rows, joins on customer_type and country
regclass = spark.read.table("silver_de.regulatory_classifications")

# The intent: the join brings in the regulatory class, THEN filter to DE
result = (platinum
    .join(regclass, ["customer_type", "country"], "inner")
    .filter(col("country") == "DE")
    .groupBy("customer_type", "region", "regulatory_class", "date")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"),
        avg("price_eur_per_kwh").alias("avg_price"),
        count("*").alias("reading_count")
    ))

result.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("silver_de.compliance_summary")

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: The Platform team configured a custom Catalyst optimization rule on this cluster six months ago — the GridUnionCountryFilterPushdown rule documented in the Platform team's runbook. The rule pushes country=X filters into nested subqueries and view definitions whenever the country column has the @regulatory_partition annotation in Unity Catalog. The annotation is present on the Platinum view's country column. The rule was added to enforce country-level data isolation for regulatory audits — once a query is scoped to one country, the rule guarantees no rows from other countries are scanned, even transiently. The new engineer has not read the runbook yet.

fields.label: Q1 — Looking at the source code, where would you expect the country='DE' filter to appear in the physical plan?
options: Above the join, since the join produces the rows that the filter then evaluates and the source code orders the filter strictly after the join call.
Below the join on the regclass scan only, since the filter references country and only the regclass side declares country in its schema.
Above the groupBy, since aggregations push their inputs through one final filter step before the partial aggregation runs over the resulting rows.
Either above or below the join depending on Catalyst's standard PushDownPredicate rule, with no platform-specific behavior in play here.
fields.correctAnswer: Either above or below the join depending on Catalyst's standard PushDownPredicate rule, with no platform-specific behavior in play here.
fields.rationale:
Without any custom rules configured, the standard Catalyst PushDownPredicate rule pushes filters past joins whenever the filtered column originates from one side and the filter is deterministic. Country is on both sides of the join in this query (the join key includes country), so the filter is push-down-eligible to both scans. The new engineer's expectation 'the filter should appear above the join because that is where the source code put it' is naive — Catalyst routinely reorders predicates relative to source code ordering. The expected behavior in vanilla Spark would be the filter pushed to the scan of whichever side has the country column declared with sufficient statistics for the planner to be confident.

When this answer is wrong: If the country column were a derived expression (e.g., a CASE statement based on region) rather than a base column, PushDownPredicate would not fire and option A would be the right answer. Here country is a plain base column on both sides.

fields.label: Q2 — Given the team_context, what actually happens in the plan and why?
options: Catalyst's standard PushDownPredicate rule pushes the filter past the join into the platinum scan; this is the same behavior any Spark cluster shows with no custom rules configured.

The custom GridUnionCountryFilterPushdown rule pushes the filter into the platinum view definition itself, before its internal joins resolve, so no non-DE rows are read from any of the underlying source tables.
AQE's runtime filter pushdown moves the filter to the latest stage where it is still valid, which on this query happens to be just before the join's hash-partitioning shuffle materialises.
The Spark planner pushes the filter to the regclass scan because the optimizer prefers to filter the smaller side of joins first, regardless of which side declares the filtered column in its schema.
fields.correctAnswer: The custom GridUnionCountryFilterPushdown rule pushes the filter into the platinum view definition itself, before its internal joins resolve, so no non-DE rows are read from any of the underlying source tables.
fields.rationale:
The custom GridUnionCountryFilterPushdown rule extends standard predicate pushdown by descending into view definitions and pushing the filter past the view's own internal joins, all the way to the original Bronze and Silver scans. The standard PushDownPredicate rule stops at the view boundary — it treats the view as a black box from a relational-algebra standpoint. The custom rule recognizes the @regulatory_partition annotation as a signal that this column has special semantics and is safe to push deeper. The result is that the underlying scans for FR, NL, and LU are never even started for a DE query.

When this answer is wrong: On a cluster where the custom rule is not configured (e.g., a new cluster spun up for ad-hoc work), only standard pushdown happens and option A would be the right diagnosis. The team_context here explicitly states the custom rule is active.

fields.label: Q3 — The new engineer is preparing to file a bug report. What should the team lead say to redirect the conversation?
options: The bug report is correct — the standard rule should not be pushing the filter that far. We should disable PushDownPredicate at the cluster level until we can investigate why it is misbehaving on this Platinum view.

The plan is doing exactly what we configured it to do via the custom rule documented in the runbook. The 'unexpected' filter position is the regulatory isolation guarantee that BNetzA requires; please read the runbook before opening bugs against optimizer behavior.

The Spark version on this cluster is older than the version the runbook describes. The custom rule was rewritten in 3.5 and the new behavior pushes filters less aggressively; we should consider rolling the cluster back to the documented version.

The Platinum view should not have been used for a single-country query; the engineer should rewrite the query against silver_de directly and that will produce a plan that matches the source code's ordering.
fields.correctAnswer: The plan is doing exactly what we configured it to do via the custom rule documented in the runbook. The 'unexpected' filter position is the regulatory isolation guarantee that BNetzA requires; please read the runbook before opening bugs against optimizer behavior.
fields.rationale:
The plan is correct relative to the platform's configuration; the engineer's surprise reflects unfamiliarity with the platform's customizations, not a bug. The team lead's response should redirect to the runbook (PSS1 lesson 7 covers configuration management; the custom rule itself is PSS2 lesson 5 territory). Filing a bug against the standard PushDownPredicate would waste cycles and risks the Platform team disabling a rule that has regulatory significance. The right cultural pattern at this scale is: when the plan looks unexpected, check the cluster's custom rules before assuming Catalyst is broken.

When this answer is wrong: If the custom rule were producing semantically incorrect output (e.g., dropping rows that should be retained), filing a bug would be exactly right. Here the rule is producing the documented behavior, so option B applies.

fields.label: Q4 — What downstream guarantee does the custom rule provide that a hand-written WHERE country='DE' inside the source code would NOT?
options: Hand-written filters are evaluated lazily, so a non-DE row could still flow through the join transiently before the filter eliminates it; the custom rule prevents transient cross-country reads from ever occurring at the scan layer.

Hand-written filters can be reordered by Catalyst to run after wide transformations whenever statistics indicate a smaller intermediate result; the custom rule pins the filter to the scan and makes its placement non-negotiable across plan rewrites.

Hand-written filters do not propagate to nested view definitions; the custom rule applies the country filter inside the Platinum view's underlying scans, which a hand-written filter at the consumer layer cannot reach without rewriting the view.
Hand-written filters are not visible in the Spark UI as a separate operator; the custom rule emits a labelled CountryFilterPushdown node that audit tools can grep for to verify regulatory isolation.
fields.correctAnswer: Hand-written filters do not propagate to nested view definitions; the custom rule applies the country filter inside the Platinum view's underlying scans, which a hand-written filter at the consumer layer cannot reach without rewriting the view.
fields.rationale:
The hand-written filter sits at the consumer layer — it filters the result of the Platinum view's join. The Platinum view's underlying joins still scan all four countries' source tables and combine them, then the filter reduces the result to DE. The custom rule pushes the country='DE' constraint into the view definition itself, so the FR, NL, and LU source tables are never scanned. For regulatory isolation (BNetzA requires evidence that no DE-scoped query touches non-DE data even transiently), the consumer-layer filter is insufficient — only scan-layer enforcement satisfies the requirement.

When this answer is wrong: If the Platinum view were defined as a UNION rather than as a JOIN across countries, a consumer-layer filter would be sufficient because each country's scan is logically independent and Catalyst could prune the unfiltered branches. The view here is defined as a JOIN over a unified meters table that contains all countries, so consumer-layer filtering does not prune the scan.

Platform-level operational note: The Platform team's runbook should be linked in the cluster's startup banner so engineers see it on first connection. GridUnion adopted this practice after three separate bug reports were filed against the GridUnionCountryFilterPushdown rule in its first two months, all by engineers who had not read the runbook.

Deferred short answer (Q5): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The Platform team is considering whether to apply the same custom rule on the Spark cluster used by the FR Analytics team. What architectural question must they answer before doing so?
→ The team must verify that the FR Analytics team's queries are uniformly single-country (no legitimate cross-country joins) AND that the @regulatory_partition annotation is present on the country columns in the FR catalogs the rule would target. If FR analysts run cross-country comparisons (e.g., DE vs FR price benchmarks), the rule would silently drop the cross-country rows by pushing a country='FR' filter where none was intended.

tasks.pssReference: PSS2 lesson 1 (Catalyst Rule Phases Deep Dive), PSS2 lesson 5 (Custom Optimizer Rules), PSS1 lesson 7 (Configuration Management at Scale), PSS8 lesson 3 (Row-Level Security).

tasks.title: AQE Skew Split Granularity Wrong for the Distribution

description.context: GridUnion Continental, the DE Customer team's Silver pipeline. The pipeline joins meter readings to a customer dimension and the join key is customer_type. Customer-type distribution at the DE level is ~60% RESIDENTIAL, 25% COMMERCIAL, 12% INDUSTRIAL, 3% PROSUMER. Within RESIDENTIAL, regional distribution is itself skewed: the NORTH region accounts for ~70% of RESIDENTIAL rows due to a recent market expansion. AQE skew handling is enabled and visibly fired in the plan, but the runtime regression the team set out to fix has only marginally improved (was 42 minutes before AQE skew handling was added; is now 31 minutes; the team expected ~12 minutes).
description.task: Read the physical plan and the per-task metrics, then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true
+- HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#42, region#11], functions=[sum(daily_kw#16), count(1)])
   +- AQEShuffleRead coalesced
      +- ShuffleQueryStage 5
         +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_type#42, region#11, 200)
            +- HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#42, region#11], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#16), partial_count(1)])
               +- SortMergeJoin(skewJoin=true) [customer_type#42], [customer_type#180], Inner
                  :- AQEShuffleRead local skewSubpartitions=24
                  :  +- ShuffleQueryStage 2
                  :     +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_type#42, 200)
                  :        +- FileScan parquet silver_de.meters_enriched [customer_type#42, region#11, daily_kw#16]
                  +- Sort [customer_type#180 ASC NULLS FIRST], false, 0
                     +- AQEShuffleRead local
                        +- ShuffleQueryStage 3
                           +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_type#180, 200)
                              +- FileScan parquet silver_de.customers [customer_type#180]

evidence.title: Stage 4 — Post-skew-split tasks reading the RESIDENTIAL sub-partitions from the meters side
rows: RESIDENTIAL sub-partition #1 — contains all NORTH-RESIDENTIAL rows

fields.label: Q1 — AQE has visibly fired — the plan shows skewJoin=true and skewSubpartitions=24 on the meters side. Why is task 4.0 still 14× slower than the other RESIDENTIAL sub-partitions?
options: AQE's skew detection ran but its split logic introduced a hash collision on customer_type, so one of the 24 sub-partitions wound up containing the bulk of the RESIDENTIAL rows by accident rather than by design.

AQE splits a skewed partition into byte-budgeted sub-partitions rather than along the within-key distribution; one sub-partition received all NORTH-RESIDENTIAL rows because their underlying hash places them on the same physical sub-partition.

AQE's split is correct but the downstream HashAggregate cannot run in parallel across the sub-partitions because the partial-aggregate output must be merged into a single stream before the final aggregate begins.

AQE's skew threshold was set too aggressively, so the split fired on a partition that did not actually need splitting; the original RESIDENTIAL distribution was uniform and the visible skew is an artefact of the split itself.
fields.correctAnswer: AQE splits a skewed partition into byte-budgeted sub-partitions rather than along the within-key distribution; one sub-partition received all NORTH-RESIDENTIAL rows because their underlying hash places them on the same physical sub-partition.
fields.rationale:
AQE's skew split operates on byte-size criteria — it identifies that one partition is much larger than the others, then splits it into N sub-partitions of roughly equal byte size. The split is hash-based on the existing partition contents; rows that already share a partition stay together unless the byte threshold forces them apart. NORTH-RESIDENTIAL rows are all RESIDENTIAL and all share the same join-key hash, so they land in the same physical partition before splitting. The splitter has no visibility into the region column — region is a payload column from its perspective, not a partitioning column — so the split cannot use region to load-balance.

When this answer is wrong: On Spark 4.0+ AQE adds an experimental cardinality-aware skew split that does sample within-key column distributions; if that flag were enabled (it is not at GridUnion as of this scenario), the split could in principle balance NORTH-RESIDENTIAL across sub-partitions. None of the productions in this track use 4.0+ features.

fields.label: Q2 — The team had hoped that 'AQE handles skew' would resolve the runtime regression. What did they misunderstand about how AQE skew handling works at the implementation level?
options: AQE skew handling only fires when both sides of the join are skewed on the same key; here only the meters side is skewed and the customers side is uniform, so AQE's split achieved less than its full potential effect.

AQE skew handling splits along physical partition byte size, not along key cardinality; within-key skew (the NORTH-RESIDENTIAL concentration inside the RESIDENTIAL key) is invisible to the splitter and survives the split intact.

AQE skew handling requires the skewJoin advisory size to be set explicitly; the cluster default is 256MB and the RESIDENTIAL partition was 200MB, so AQE technically did not classify it as skewed and the split did not run.

AQE skew handling is implemented as a cost-based heuristic and disables itself when the optimizer cannot estimate the post-split partition sizes accurately, which happens whenever statistics on the join key are missing or stale at the catalog level.
fields.correctAnswer: AQE skew handling splits along physical partition byte size, not along key cardinality; within-key skew (the NORTH-RESIDENTIAL concentration inside the RESIDENTIAL key) is invisible to the splitter and survives the split intact.
fields.rationale:
AQE skew handling is parameterized by partition byte size and partition factor (typically: a partition is skewed if it is more than 5× the median partition size AND larger than the advisory skew partition size). The metric is byte size, never key distribution. This means AQE handles between-key skew (one key has more rows than other keys) but not within-key skew (one key contains internally non-uniform rows). The team's mental model was 'AQE handles skew' as a complete statement; the precise statement is 'AQE handles skew of the partition byte distribution at shuffle time'.

When this answer is wrong: Option A would be correct in a scenario where AQE fired the split but the customers side was such that the joined output of one sub-partition was disproportionately large (the build side cardinality matters too). Here the customers side is small and uniform, so the only mystery is on the meters side.

fields.label: Q3 — Identify the architectural intervention that would actually resolve this. Code-level changes are not sufficient — the join key has the within-key skew built into it.
options: Tune spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor to a smaller value (default is 5) so AQE classifies smaller asymmetries as skewed and splits more aggressively, which will catch the within-key skew on the next run.

Pre-aggregate by region within each customer_type into an intermediate Gold table; the downstream join then keys on (customer_type, region) and the within-key skew of NORTH-RESIDENTIAL becomes a separate partition AQE can isolate.

Replace the join with a manual broadcast of the customers table; even though customers is over the broadcast threshold, broadcasting it eliminates the join shuffle entirely and bypasses the skew problem at its source.

Increase the default shuffle partition count from 200 to 2000; the larger denominator distributes the RESIDENTIAL rows across more physical partitions so each sub-partition's NORTH share becomes proportionally smaller.
fields.correctAnswer: Pre-aggregate by region within each customer_type into an intermediate Gold table; the downstream join then keys on (customer_type, region) and the within-key skew of NORTH-RESIDENTIAL becomes a separate partition AQE can isolate.
fields.rationale:
The architectural fix is to make the within-key skew visible to the partitioner. Pre-aggregating by region within each customer_type produces an intermediate table where (customer_type, region) is the natural key. The downstream join can then partition on that compound key, and AQE's standard byte-size skew split CAN isolate NORTH-RESIDENTIAL into its own partition because (RESIDENTIAL, NORTH) is now a distinct partition from (RESIDENTIAL, SOUTH). The composite-key strategy is the canonical PSS4 approach to within-key skew. Tuning AQE parameters (option A) does not help — the parameter changes affect when the split fires, not how it splits. Manual broadcast (option C) creates a driver memory risk. Increasing partitions (option D) does not change the hash bucket the NORTH-RESIDENTIAL rows land in.

When this answer is wrong: If the customer_type were a leaf-level key with no further structure (e.g., a randomized hash that has no semantic sub-grouping), pre-aggregation would not help and salting would be the next architectural option. Here customer_type has a clean sub-structure (region) that pre-aggregation can exploit.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level remedy is a 'within-key skew detector' added to the pipeline observability layer. It runs per-shuffle-stage and computes the coefficient of variation of task durations within each AQE-split stage; values above 0.5 indicate the split did not balance the work and trigger an alert recommending architectural review (composite key, salting, or pre-aggregation).

Deferred short answer (Q4): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The team is reluctant to introduce the intermediate pre-aggregation because it adds a Gold table they must own and maintain. What single piece of monitoring would let them stop reaching for AQE first and reach for the architectural fix sooner on similar future incidents?
→ A task-duration-variance metric within each AQE-split shuffle stage: if AQE split a partition into N sub-partitions and the duration variance across those sub-partitions exceeds, say, 3×, the split did not actually balance the work. This is a within-key skew signal that AQE itself cannot detect because it only measures bytes. Track it per pipeline-per-stage and alert when the variance crosses the threshold.

tasks.pssReference: PSS2 lesson 4 (AQE Runtime Adaptation Mechanics — skew split implementation), PSS4 lesson 6 (Skew-Resistant Join Design — composite key strategy), PSS6 lesson 5 (The Shuffle Manager). Cross-track: JAI4.2 introduced AQE skew detection at recognition level; this exercise is its senior-level extension to the case AQE cannot fix.

tasks.title: Bucket Misalignment Across Teams

description.context: GridUnion Continental, DE deployment. The Analytics team's daily aggregation joins the Silver meters table to the Customer team's Silver customer dimension. Both tables are bucketed; the bucketed-join optimization should fire and skip the shuffle. The plan instead shows a full SortMergeJoin with shuffles on both sides. Each team confirms their own bucketing is correct; both buckets are correct in isolation. The runtime is 18 minutes; the team had targeted 6 minutes once bucketing was in place.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — the team_context block names the operational pressures in play — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, count

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName("daily_customer_aggregate")
    .config("spark.sql.sources.bucketing.enabled", "true")
    .config("spark.sql.bucketing.coalesceBucketsInJoin.enabled", "true")
    .getOrCreate())

# silver_de.meters: bucketed 256-ways on meter_id
# (chosen by the Analytics team to optimize their per-meter rolling-window queries)
meters = spark.read.table("silver_de.meters")

# silver_de.customers: bucketed 128-ways on customer_id
# (chosen by the Customer team to optimize their per-customer profile queries)
customers = spark.read.table("silver_de.customers")

# The join — Analytics expected the bucketed-join optimization to fire
result = (meters
    .join(customers, on="customer_id", how="inner")
    .groupBy("customer_type", "region", "date")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"),
        avg("price_eur_per_kwh").alias("avg_price"),
        count("*").alias("reading_count")
    ))

result.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("silver_de.daily_customer_aggregate")

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: The Analytics team owns silver_de.meters and bucketed it 256-ways on meter_id to make their primary query — a per-meter 30-day rolling window — fast. The Customer team owns silver_de.customers and bucketed it 128-ways on customer_id to make their primary query — a per-customer profile lookup — fast. Both bucketing decisions were made independently 18 months ago, before the two teams collaborated on the daily aggregate. There is no shared bucket registry on the DE platform. The Customer team's quarterly maintenance window is in three weeks; the Analytics team's is in six weeks; both teams require ~45 minutes of read-blocked time for an OPTIMIZE that re-buckets a Silver table at this scale.

fields.label: Q1 — Why does the bucketed-join optimization not fire here, given that both tables are bucketed and the bucketing config is enabled?
options: The bucketed-join optimization requires both tables to be bucketed on the same column with matching bucket counts; meters is bucketed on meter_id (256 buckets) and customers on customer_id (128 buckets), which fails both criteria simultaneously.

The bucketed-join optimization requires the bucket count to be a prime number on at least one side; 256 and 128 are both powers of two, so the optimization is suppressed by Catalyst's default bucket-coalescing rule.

The bucketed-join optimization requires statistics to be refreshed within the last 24 hours on both tables; the customers table was last analyzed three weeks ago and the optimization defaults to off when statistics are stale.

The bucketed-join optimization is disabled by default in Spark 3.5; the spark.sql.sources.bucketing.enabled flag controls bucket-aware reads, not the join optimization, which requires its own separate configuration property.
fields.correctAnswer: The bucketed-join optimization requires both tables to be bucketed on the same column with matching bucket counts; meters is bucketed on meter_id (256 buckets) and customers on customer_id (128 buckets), which fails both criteria simultaneously.
fields.rationale:
The bucketed-join optimization requires three conditions simultaneously: (1) both tables bucketed on the join key column, (2) matching bucket counts (or counts that share a power-of-two ratio for coalescing), and (3) the same hash function. Meters here is bucketed on meter_id, not on the join key customer_id, so condition 1 fails immediately. Even if both tables were on customer_id, the count mismatch (256 vs 128) would require bucket coalescing, which can sometimes bridge it. With the column mismatch, no amount of count alignment helps.

When this answer is wrong: On Spark 4.0+ a 'flexible bucketing' proposal would bucket on multiple keys simultaneously, but that proposal has not landed and is not what this scenario covers.

fields.label: Q2 — What bucket layout would have made the bucketed-join optimization fire for this specific join?
options: Both tables bucketed on (customer_id, meter_id) with the same bucket count, so that any future join on either column finds bucket alignment without a shuffle on either side of the join condition.
Both tables bucketed on customer_id with the same bucket count (e.g., 256 each), so the join key matches and rows for the same customer_id land in the same bucket on both sides.
The customers table re-bucketed on meter_id with 256 buckets, matching the meters table's existing bucket count and column, so the optimization reads from the meters bucket layout natively.
Both tables left unbucketed, with a Z-ORDER on customer_id applied at write time; Z-ORDER produces sorted runs that the bucketed-join optimization can detect at the file-statistics layer.
fields.correctAnswer: Both tables bucketed on customer_id with the same bucket count (e.g., 256 each), so the join key matches and rows for the same customer_id land in the same bucket on both sides.
fields.rationale:
Bucketing both tables on customer_id with matching bucket counts (256 each, or 128 each — equal counts being the cleanest) makes rows with the same customer_id deterministically land in the same bucket on both sides. Spark can then read corresponding buckets from each side without shuffling, hash-merge them locally, and produce the join output in parallel across all buckets. Option A (compound key bucketing) is supported but adds complexity for marginal benefit on a single join key. Option C inverts the problem — meters has 256 buckets on meter_id, and the join key is customer_id, so re-bucketing customers on meter_id makes no sense. Option D conflates Z-ORDER (a Delta-specific data skipping feature) with bucketing.

When this answer is wrong: If the Analytics team's rolling-window query is dramatically more important than the cross-team join (option in scenarios where the rolling window runs every five minutes and the cross-team join runs nightly), keeping meters on meter_id and accepting the shuffle on the cross-team join is the correct trade-off.

fields.label: Q3 — Each team's bucketing decision was correct for that team's primary query. Why did neither team choose the bucket layout that would benefit this specific join?
options: The Analytics team rejected customer_id bucketing because it would degrade their rolling-window query, which partitions by meter_id and requires bucket-key alignment with the partition key for the window-function fast path to fire on each meter.

The Customer team rejected meter_id bucketing because their profile-lookup query joins to a separate per-customer subscription history table on customer_id, and bucketing on meter_id would require that subscription table to also re-bucket.

Each team optimized for its own primary query, not for the cross-team join; meters benefits from meter_id bucketing for the rolling window, customers benefits from customer_id bucketing for the profile lookup, and no single bucket key serves both queries plus this join.

Catalyst's bucket-coalescing rule (since Spark 3.1) was assumed to bridge the mismatch automatically by reducing 256 buckets to 128 at read time; the rule does fire but only when the bucket counts have a power-of-two ratio AND share the same key.
fields.correctAnswer: Each team optimized for its own primary query, not for the cross-team join; meters benefits from meter_id bucketing for the rolling window, customers benefits from customer_id bucketing for the profile lookup, and no single bucket key serves both queries plus this join.
fields.rationale:
Each team's bucketing decision was locally optimal — and there is no shared bucket key that would simultaneously serve all three queries (rolling window on meters, profile lookup on customers, daily aggregate join). Bucketing has exactly one key per table; choosing it is a single-shot decision with multi-query consequences. Without cross-team coordination at design time, the locally-optimal choices produce a globally-suboptimal cross-team join. This is the canonical multi-team architectural problem the senior track surfaces — and the platform's response is structural, not technical.

When this answer is wrong: If a single bucket key WOULD serve both teams' primary queries (e.g., both teams' workload routes through customer_id), then the local-optimum framing is wrong and the cause is just lack of awareness. The scenario states explicitly that meter_id and customer_id serve different primary queries, ruling that out.

fields.label: Q4 — What platform-level architectural decision would prevent this class of misalignment from recurring as new cross-team joins emerge?
options: Mandate that all Silver tables in the DE catalog be bucketed on customer_id with 256 buckets; this gives every cross-team join a guaranteed alignment at the cost of degrading per-table queries that prefer a different key.

Disable bucketing entirely on Silver tables and rely on AQE to handle skew at runtime; this avoids the alignment problem at the cost of reintroducing the shuffle that bucketing was meant to eliminate in the first place.

Establish a shared bucket registry that records each table's bucket key and count, and require any new cross-team join to be validated against the registry; teams can keep their primary-key buckets and add a secondary alignment policy by exception.

Move the join logic into a Platinum view maintained by the Platform team; the view materializes a pre-joined result whose bucket layout is owned centrally and stays aligned independent of either source team's table evolution.
fields.correctAnswer: Establish a shared bucket registry that records each table's bucket key and count, and require any new cross-team join to be validated against the registry; teams can keep their primary-key buckets and add a secondary alignment policy by exception.
fields.rationale:
A shared bucket registry is the platform-level pattern from PSS4 lesson 2. It does not mandate a single bucket key (option A would over-constrain teams whose primary queries genuinely benefit from different keys). It does not give up on bucketing (option B would pay the shuffle cost forever). It does not centralize the join (option D solves this one join but does not generalize). The registry pattern records each table's bucket layout, makes cross-team consumers aware of what alignment exists, and lets the Platform team add coordination by exception — when a cross-team join becomes important enough, the registry tells the platform team which secondary alignment to add (e.g., add a customer_id-bucketed clone of meters as a separate table, or add a bucket alignment clause to the data contract).

When this answer is wrong: On a small platform (one or two teams), the registry overhead exceeds its benefit and option A's mandated single key is more practical. The threshold is roughly five tables and three teams; below that, registry overhead dominates.

fields.label: Q5 — The Analytics team proposes that the Customer team re-bucket their customers table on customer_id with 256 buckets to match the meters table. What is wrong with this proposal as the right architectural fix?
options: The customers table is already bucketed on customer_id; only the bucket count differs (128 vs 256), and Spark's bucket coalescing rule reads 256-bucket tables as 128 buckets transparently when the join requires alignment, so no re-bucket is needed at all.

Re-bucketing the customers table breaks the Customer team's profile-lookup query, which is tuned for the 128-bucket layout; the proposal optimizes for the cross-team join at the cost of regressing the Customer team's primary workload that runs ten times more often.

Re-bucketing requires a 45-minute read-block on the customers table during OPTIMIZE; the Customer team has a quarterly maintenance window in three weeks and the proposal is feasible operationally but blocks all consumers for the duration of that window.

The proposal is correct in principle but wrong in direction; the meters table has the larger bucket count, so re-bucketing the smaller customers table to 256 is the standard approach and does not regress any consumer's query as long as it remains a power of two.
fields.correctAnswer: Re-bucketing the customers table breaks the Customer team's profile-lookup query, which is tuned for the 128-bucket layout; the proposal optimizes for the cross-team join at the cost of regressing the Customer team's primary workload that runs ten times more often.
fields.rationale:
The Customer team's primary workload (profile lookup) runs frequently and is tuned for the 128-bucket layout. Re-bucketing to 256 changes the per-bucket data volume, the file count, the parallelism characteristic of the lookup, and may regress its runtime substantially. The Analytics team's proposal externalizes the cost of the cross-team join onto the Customer team's far more frequent workload. The right architectural conversation involves quantifying both impacts (cross-team join savings vs profile-lookup regression) and deciding which trade-off the platform prefers, ideally with the Platform team mediating. Option C identifies a real operational concern (the maintenance window) but it is secondary to the workload-impact concern.

When this answer is wrong: If the cross-team join were dramatically more critical (e.g., a regulatory-deadline aggregation) AND the profile-lookup regression were small (e.g., 200ms to 250ms is irrelevant for an interactive lookup), the Analytics proposal could be the right call after all. Quantification is what distinguishes the cases.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level monitoring rule that catches this class of issue early is a 'bucket alignment' check that runs on every new cross-team join PR. The check parses the join condition, looks up each table's bucket layout in the registry, and warns at code review time when alignment will not fire. GridUnion's DE platform team added this check after this exact incident; it now runs in the data-engineering CI pipeline.

Deferred short answer (Q6): Short answer (1–2 sentences). Spark 3.1+ has a bucket coalescing feature (spark.sql.bucketing.coalesceBucketsInJoin.enabled is set to true in the snippet). Why does it not save us here?
→ Bucket coalescing in Spark 3.1+ handles bucket count mismatches when both tables are bucketed on the SAME key — for example, 256 buckets on customer_id can be coalesced down to read as 128 buckets when joined with a 128-bucket-on-customer_id table. It does not handle key mismatches: meter_id buckets and customer_id buckets are not the same logical partitioning at all, so there is nothing to coalesce. The flag does fire, but it sees no eligible pair.

tasks.pssReference: PSS4 lesson 2 (Bucketed Joins at Enterprise Scale — shared bucket registry pattern), PSS1 lesson 3 (Table Ownership and Access Contracts), PSS5 lesson 3 (Data Contracts — bucket-layout clauses).

tasks.title: Pre-Aggregated Gold Table Stale by Design

description.context: GridUnion Continental, DE deployment. The Analytics team owns a Gold pre-aggregated table gold_de.daily_meter_by_region, refreshed by a nightly Airflow DAG at 02:00. The Billing team consumes it in their own pipeline that runs at 07:00 daily and writes the per-region invoice line items. The Analytics team's own dashboard reads the same Gold table at 06:00 daily for the morning ops review. On Wednesday, the dashboard reported a number that 'looked off' — the Analytics lead investigated and found the Gold table held Tuesday's value rather than Wednesday's. The Billing team's 07:00 run looked correct. No code or schedule changes were made on Wednesday. The Analytics dashboard's morning review is now blocked pending root-cause; the Billing team is not impacted.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — the team_context block names the operational pressures in play — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
# Pipeline: gold_de_daily_meter_by_region
# Owner: Analytics team
# Schedule: Airflow DAG, daily at 02:00 UTC

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, count, current_date, date_sub

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("gold_de_daily_meter_by_region").getOrCreate()

# Reads from silver_de.meters_enriched, which is itself refreshed by
# the Platform team's Bronze-to-Silver pipeline. Silver pipeline runs
# whenever new Bronze CSVs arrive — typically 23:30 to 04:30, with
# completion timestamps that vary day to day depending on Bronze volume.
silver = spark.read.table("silver_de.meters_enriched")

# Aggregate yesterday only — the assumption is that everything for
# yesterday is now in Silver by the time this 02:00 job starts.
yesterday = (silver
    .filter(col("date") == date_sub(current_date(), 1))
    .groupBy("region", "customer_type", "date")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"),
        avg("price_eur_per_kwh").alias("avg_price"),
        count("*").alias("reading_count")
    ))

# Append into the Gold table (partitioned by date)
yesterday.write.mode("append").partitionBy("date").parquet("gold_de/daily_meter_by_region/")

# Note: this pipeline does NOT verify that Silver is complete for
# 'yesterday' before reading. The 02:00 schedule was chosen 18 months ago
# based on an empirical average of Silver completion at 01:30.

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: Three teams interact with this Gold table on a daily cadence. The Platform team owns silver_de.meters_enriched and refreshes it from Bronze whenever new CSVs arrive — completion typically lands between 01:00 and 02:30 but is variable. The Analytics team owns gold_de.daily_meter_by_region and refreshes it at 02:00 nightly via Airflow, then reads it at 06:00 for the dashboard. The Billing team reads the same Gold table at 07:00 for their own downstream aggregate. On the Wednesday in question, Bronze arrival was unusually delayed and the Silver refresh did not complete until 02:18 — eighteen minutes after the Gold pipeline had already started reading. There is no completion-event signaling between Platform and Analytics; the schedule-based assumption is the only coordination. The Analytics team has been considering tightening the 02:00 schedule to 03:00 but had not done so.

fields.label: Q1 — What did the Gold pipeline read on Wednesday morning, and what did it write to the Gold table?
options: It read the Silver table mid-refresh and wrote a partially aggregated Tuesday partition; the dashboard saw Tuesday's value because Wednesday's row had not yet appeared in Silver when the Gold filter on date='yesterday' ran.

It read a fully complete Silver table for Tuesday's date partition (the only date the filter selects), but the underlying Silver Tuesday partition itself was incomplete because the Bronze-to-Silver job had not finished writing all of Tuesday's late-arriving readings yet.

It read Wednesday's Silver partition successfully, but the Gold append failed silently due to a partition-write conflict with the Platform team's still-running Silver job, leaving the Gold table at its previous Tuesday state.

It read the Silver table as expected, but Catalyst's date_sub(current_date(), 1) was evaluated at the driver in UTC while the Gold partition column is in CET, which on Wednesday's clock change shifted the filter back by an extra day and produced Tuesday's data instead of Wednesday's.
fields.correctAnswer: It read a fully complete Silver table for Tuesday's date partition (the only date the filter selects), but the underlying Silver Tuesday partition itself was incomplete because the Bronze-to-Silver job had not finished writing all of Tuesday's late-arriving readings yet.
fields.rationale:
The Gold pipeline filters on yesterday's date — at 02:00 on Wednesday, that is Tuesday's date. The filter ran correctly and selected Tuesday's partition from Silver. The defect is that Tuesday's partition was itself incomplete: the Bronze-to-Silver job for Tuesday had not finished writing the full set of Tuesday's readings (Bronze was delayed and Silver wasn't done until 02:18). Gold therefore aggregated a partial Tuesday and wrote that partial result. The dashboard at 06:00 saw a Tuesday total that was lower than the eventually-correct Tuesday total, which is what 'looked off'. Wednesday's data was not the issue here at all.

When this answer is wrong: If the Gold pipeline had filtered on current_date() instead of date_sub(current_date(),1), option A would be the right diagnosis (mid-refresh of an incomplete day's data). The snippet's filter is on yesterday, so the issue is yesterday's incomplete Silver partition.

fields.label: Q2 — Why did the Billing team's 07:00 pipeline appear correct while the 06:00 dashboard saw stale data?
options: The Billing team's 07:00 job overrode the Gold table's stale partition with a corrected value computed from Silver directly, masking the upstream issue from any Billing-only consumer of the result.

By 07:00 the Analytics team had been alerted to the stale Gold partition and re-ran the Gold pipeline manually; the Billing job consumed the corrected Gold table and the dashboard read the Gold table before the manual rerun completed.

Spark's caching layer expired the Gold table's partition between the 06:00 dashboard read and the 07:00 Billing read, so the Billing read went straight to the underlying file system and saw a more current state than the cached view.

The Billing team reads from the Silver table directly for any partition where the Gold table is missing or empty, treating Gold as a hot cache rather than as a source of truth, so the upstream issue is masked from Billing's perspective.
fields.correctAnswer: The Billing team reads from the Silver table directly for any partition where the Gold table is missing or empty, treating Gold as a hot cache rather than as a source of truth, so the upstream issue is masked from Billing's perspective.
fields.rationale:
The Billing team's pattern is to read Silver directly when Gold is missing or known-stale, and only use Gold as a cache when it is current. This is a defensive pattern that a downstream-resilient consumer adopts; it masks upstream incidents from that consumer's perspective. The Analytics dashboard, by contrast, reads Gold unconditionally and trusts whatever is there. The asymmetry between the two consumers reflects different levels of investment in defensive consumption — and it is why the dashboard surfaced the issue while the Billing pipeline did not. The Billing team's defensive read is itself architectural; the dashboard's trusting read is also architectural, just in the opposite direction.

When this answer is wrong: If the Billing pipeline had genuinely consumed the stale Gold value (rather than falling through to Silver), option B (a manual re-run between 06:00 and 07:00) would be the right diagnosis. The team_context implies Billing has a recovery path, making D the actual mechanism.

fields.label: Q3 — Identify the implicit architectural commitment the Gold pipeline made — the assumption that produced this incident.
options: The pipeline assumed Silver is always complete by 02:00 because the historical mean Silver completion time is 01:30; this is a schedule-based coordination model with no data-availability check on the upstream's actual completion state.

The pipeline assumed Spark's transaction layer would block the Gold read until the Silver write completed; this is a transaction-ordering assumption that holds for Delta tables but not for the Parquet tables in use here on the DE platform.

The pipeline assumed AQE's runtime adaptation would detect the partial Silver state and either delay the read or skip the incomplete partition; this is a runtime-coordination assumption that AQE does not actually provide for cross-pipeline reads.

The pipeline assumed Airflow would suspend the Gold DAG when its upstream Silver DAG was still running; this is an orchestration-coordination assumption that requires explicit dependency configuration in the Airflow scheduler.
fields.correctAnswer: The pipeline assumed Silver is always complete by 02:00 because the historical mean Silver completion time is 01:30; this is a schedule-based coordination model with no data-availability check on the upstream's actual completion state.
fields.rationale:
The architectural commitment is the schedule-as-coordination pattern: the 02:00 Gold schedule was chosen 18 months ago based on the historical mean Silver completion at 01:30, with an implicit 30-minute buffer. The commitment is correct for the average case and silently broken whenever Silver runs longer than expected. The defect is not Bronze's delay (which will recur), nor a transaction-layer assumption (these are Parquet writes, not Delta), nor an AQE assumption (AQE handles intra-query adaptation, not cross-pipeline timing). It is the absence of any data-availability check at all — Gold trusts the schedule rather than the data.

When this answer is wrong: On a Delta-based platform with strict transactional ordering across pipelines (rare but possible), option B could be a defensible diagnosis. The DE platform here uses Parquet; Delta is on the senior platform's adoption roadmap but not yet deployed.

fields.label: Q4 — The Analytics team is debating two fixes. Fix 1: move the Gold schedule from 02:00 to 03:00 and add monitoring. Fix 2: replace the schedule with an event-driven trigger fired by the Silver pipeline's completion. Which is the senior-level correct answer?
options: Fix 1, with monitoring set to alert when Silver completion is later than 03:00. The schedule shift handles the common case and the alert handles the tail; this matches the operational maturity of most data platforms and avoids the orchestration complexity of event-driven triggering.

Fix 2, an event-driven trigger fired by Silver completion. The schedule fix is a deferral of the same architectural defect; events scale to all future timing variations without requiring a schedule revisit each time, which is the senior-level correct pattern for cross-pipeline coordination.

Either fix is acceptable in isolation; the senior decision is the COMBINATION — event-driven trigger as the primary mechanism with the schedule retained as a fallback safety net at 04:00 in case the event signal is dropped, plus monitoring on both paths.

Neither fix is correct; the right architectural answer is to remove the Gold pre-aggregation entirely and have the dashboard query Silver directly, eliminating the cross-pipeline coordination problem at its source by removing one of the two pipelines that must coordinate.
fields.correctAnswer: Either fix is acceptable in isolation; the senior decision is the COMBINATION — event-driven trigger as the primary mechanism with the schedule retained as a fallback safety net at 04:00 in case the event signal is dropped, plus monitoring on both paths.
fields.rationale:
Fix 1 alone defers the architectural defect to a later (eventually inevitable) Silver delay past 03:00. Fix 2 alone is correct architecturally but introduces a new failure mode — what happens when the event signal is lost, duplicated, or never fires due to an upstream issue. The senior pattern is event-driven primary plus a schedule-based fallback (a 'safety net' run at, say, 04:00 that fires only if the event-driven trigger has not fired). Monitoring covers both paths. This is the same pattern as a primary database with a periodic snapshot — the snapshot is not the system of record, but it bounds the worst case when the primary fails.

When this answer is wrong: On a small platform where event-driven infrastructure does not yet exist, option A is the operationally correct interim choice; option C is correct as a target state but may not be feasible as a Day-1 fix. The judgment is whether the platform's orchestration system already supports event-driven triggers.

fields.label: Q5 — Suppose the team adopts Fix 2 (event-driven trigger). What new architectural concern does this introduce that the schedule-based design did not have?
options: Event-driven triggering ties the Gold pipeline's reliability to the Silver pipeline's completion-event reliability; if the event signal is dropped or duplicated, the Gold pipeline either skips a day or runs twice, neither of which the schedule-based design exposed.

Event-driven triggering requires the Silver pipeline's completion event to carry a schema version, so that schema-incompatible Silver outputs do not fire the Gold trigger; this adds a contract between the Platform team and the Analytics team that did not previously exist.

Event-driven triggering causes the Gold pipeline to run during cluster-busy hours rather than the off-peak 02:00 window; resource contention with daytime workloads becomes a new scheduling concern that the original off-peak design avoided by construction.

Event-driven triggering removes the Analytics team's ability to manually re-run the Gold pipeline ad-hoc; the trigger fires only on Silver completion, and any out-of-band rerun must instead be performed via direct backfill scripts owned by the Platform team.
fields.correctAnswer: Event-driven triggering ties the Gold pipeline's reliability to the Silver pipeline's completion-event reliability; if the event signal is dropped or duplicated, the Gold pipeline either skips a day or runs twice, neither of which the schedule-based design exposed.
fields.rationale:
Event-driven coordination shifts the failure mode from 'schedule-vs-actual completion mismatch' to 'event delivery reliability'. If the Silver completion event is dropped (network glitch, scheduler restart, event bus outage), the Gold trigger never fires and the pipeline silently does not run. If the event fires twice (retry without idempotency), the Gold pipeline runs twice and may produce duplicate writes. Both failure modes are absent from the schedule-based design (which always runs and always once). The combined design (Fix 2 plus a schedule-based safety net) addresses this by making the schedule the de-facto idempotency-backed fallback. Option B is a real concern but is a one-time contract setup, not a recurring operational risk; options C and D are not consequential at this scale.

When this answer is wrong: If the platform's event bus is itself a Spark-managed Delta table (event sourcing model), option B becomes the dominant concern; the team would design schema versioning into the event itself. GridUnion's event bus is a managed cloud service, making option A the dominant concern.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level monitoring rule that catches this is a per-table 'completeness check' that compares row counts in the latest partition against historical means and alerts when the count is more than 2 standard deviations below expected. GridUnion's DE platform team added this check after the same incident class recurred three times in 2025. It runs on every Silver table within five minutes of the partition's first write.

Deferred short answer (Q6): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The Analytics team's root cause document blames the Bronze delay on Wednesday. Why is that the wrong root cause?
→ Bronze delays are a recurring fact of the platform — capacity events, network issues, and incremental volume growth all push completion later. Blaming Wednesday's Bronze delay treats this Wednesday's specific event as the cause; the actual cause is the architectural commitment that Gold's schedule assumes Silver has completed. The next Bronze delay (which will happen) will trigger the same incident.

tasks.pssReference: PSS1 lesson 6 (Cross-Team Pipeline Dependencies — event-driven coordination), PSS10 lesson 4 (Platform SLA Governance — freshness contracts), PSS10 lesson 5 (Incident Management for Data Platforms — root cause discipline).

tasks.title: Z-ORDER Column Defeated by Predicate Order

description.context: GridUnion Continental, the BNetzA quarterly audit aggregation. The DE Analytics team Z-ORDERed their three-year archive Delta table on (country, region, date) at the last quarterly OPTIMIZE, expecting their typical audit query (filter by country and date) to skip ~95% of files based on the Z-ORDER's data-skipping statistics. The audit query runs in 32 minutes; the team budgeted 8 minutes assuming the expected file skipping. The plan shows that 60% of the files were read despite both filters being present.
description.task: Read the physical plan and the per-task metrics, then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true
+- HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#42], functions=[sum(daily_kw#16), count(1)])
   +- AQEShuffleRead coalesced
      +- ShuffleQueryStage 1
         +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_type#42, 200)
            +- HashAggregate(keys=[customer_type#42], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#16), partial_count(1)])
               +- Project [customer_type#42, daily_kw#16]
                  +- Filter ((country#9 = DE) AND ((date#15 >= 2026-01-01) AND (date#15 <= 2026-03-31)))
                     +- FileScan parquet silver_de.meters_archive [customer_type#42, daily_kw#16, country#9, date#15]
                        Location: PreparedDeltaFileIndex(1 paths)[abfss://gridunion@.../silver_de/meters_archive]
                        PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(country), EqualTo(country,DE), IsNotNull(date), GreaterThanOrEqual(date,2026-01-01), LessThanOrEqual(date,2026-03-31)]
                        ReadSchema: struct<customer_type:string,daily_kw:double,country:string,date:date>
                        DataFilters: [(country#9 = DE), (date#15 >= 2026-01-01), (date#15 <= 2026-03-31)]
                        ZOrderBy: [country, region, date]

evidence.title: Stage 0 — FileScan tasks reading the Z-ORDERed Delta table; expected ~5% of files but actually reading 60%

fields.label: Q1 — The Z-ORDER is on (country, region, date) and the query filters on country and date. Why are so many files being read?
options: Z-ORDER's data skipping uses min/max statistics per file, and adjacent Z-ORDER values produce overlapping min/max ranges that defeat the prune; this is a known limitation when the Z-ORDER values cluster densely on a single column.

Z-ORDER skipping is column-prefix sensitive: the country filter prunes correctly, but the date filter cannot prune because region (the column between country and date in the Z-ORDER) is absent from the query, leaving date's pruning ineffective on most files.

Delta's data skipping is disabled by default for tables larger than 1 TB unless spark.databricks.delta.stats.skipping.enabled is explicitly set to true in the cluster configuration for archive workloads.

The query reads the table via the FileScan parquet operator rather than the Delta operator, which loses access to the Delta transaction log's statistics; the PushedFilters are evaluated at scan time but cannot drive file-level skipping.
fields.correctAnswer: Z-ORDER skipping is column-prefix sensitive: the country filter prunes correctly, but the date filter cannot prune because region (the column between country and date in the Z-ORDER) is absent from the query, leaving date's pruning ineffective on most files.
fields.rationale:
Z-ORDER produces a space-filling curve that orders rows on disk according to the chosen column tuple, and data skipping reads min/max statistics per file. The skipping is most effective for the FIRST column in the Z-ORDER tuple, somewhat effective for later columns when earlier columns are also constrained, and ineffective for late columns when intermediate columns are unconstrained. Here the query filters on country (the first column — prunes correctly to DE files) and date (the third column). Region is the second column and is not in the query, so date's per-file min/max ranges overlap heavily across files within DE — every DE file likely contains rows from many dates, since the Z-ORDER interleaves by region within country before refining by date. The prefix-prefix-skipping behavior of Z-ORDER is exactly this.

When this answer is wrong: On Delta with liquid clustering (Spark 3.5+ / Delta 3.x), the column-prefix dependency is much weaker because clustering adapts to query patterns. GridUnion's DE platform is on Delta 2.4, where Z-ORDER's prefix-sensitivity is in full effect.

fields.label: Q2 — Which of these query rewrites would actually improve file skipping without changing the Z-ORDER layout?
options: Add a region filter to the WHERE clause; even a permissive range like region IN ('NORTH','SOUTH','EAST','WEST') gives Z-ORDER skipping the prefix it needs to evaluate the date predicate against per-file statistics.

Replace the date BETWEEN predicate with two separate filters (date >= and date <=) chained as separate transformations; Catalyst's predicate pushdown handles chained filters more aggressively than a single BETWEEN expression.

Cast the date column to a timestamp before filtering; Z-ORDER skipping uses timestamp-typed min/max statistics with finer granularity than date-typed statistics, which gives the file pruner more precision.

Wrap the filter in a CTE (WITH clause) and project country and date as the only columns selected; Catalyst recognizes CTE-projected columns as candidates for aggressive pruning that ordinary projections do not unlock.
fields.correctAnswer: Add a region filter to the WHERE clause; even a permissive range like region IN ('NORTH','SOUTH','EAST','WEST') gives Z-ORDER skipping the prefix it needs to evaluate the date predicate against per-file statistics.
fields.rationale:
Adding a permissive region filter — even one that matches all values — gives the Z-ORDER skipper the second-column constraint it needs to evaluate the date predicate per-file. The mechanism: with country=DE AND region IN (...), the skipper can use the (country, region) prefix to identify a small subset of files, then within that subset the date min/max statistics become tighter (each file in the prefix-pruned subset covers a narrow date range). Without the region constraint, the skipper sees that within DE, files span all regions and therefore all dates; date pruning is therefore ineffective. This is counterintuitive — adding a 'permissive' filter actually accelerates the query — but it is correct for Z-ORDER's prefix-skipping mechanics.

When this answer is wrong: If the region values were not enumerable cheaply (e.g., 10,000 regions rather than 4), this rewrite is impractical. With four DE regions known statically, it is the cheapest fix available.

fields.label: Q3 — Identify the architectural fix the team can apply at the table layout level (without changing the audit query). Name the operational cost that comes with it.
options: Re-Z-ORDER on (country, date, region); region moves to the end of the prefix and the audit query's date predicate now has the prefix it needs. Operational cost: a one-time OPTIMIZE that read-blocks the table for ~45 minutes during which the Billing team cannot read.

Add a partition column on country at the Delta partition level (partitioned by country, Z-ORDER on date); partition pruning is more aggressive than Z-ORDER skipping and removes the prefix-dependency entirely. Operational cost: a full rewrite of the table at ~3 hours of read-block time.

Increase Delta's spark.databricks.delta.optimize.maxFileSize to merge small files into larger ones, so each file's min/max range is wider and skipping is more selective per file. Operational cost: an ongoing OPTIMIZE that runs nightly with a small ~5 minute read-block.

Replace the Z-ORDER with a liquid clustering layout (Spark 3.5 / Delta 2.4 feature); liquid clustering automatically chooses the right column ordering for the query workload. Operational cost: a migration that requires Delta Lake 3.x — one major version above the platform's current 2.4.
fields.correctAnswer: Re-Z-ORDER on (country, date, region); region moves to the end of the prefix and the audit query's date predicate now has the prefix it needs. Operational cost: a one-time OPTIMIZE that read-blocks the table for ~45 minutes during which the Billing team cannot read.
fields.rationale:
Re-Z-ORDERing on (country, date, region) gives the audit query a clean prefix: country (filtered) → date (filtered) → region (unconstrained). This is the layout that matches the audit query's WHERE pattern. The cost is the OPTIMIZE — Delta's Z-ORDER OPTIMIZE rewrites all the data, which at the archive's size is roughly 45 minutes of read-block time (during which the Billing team's Z-ORDERed-table reads will either fail or block). The senior architectural conversation is whether the audit's recurring 32-minute runtime versus the proposed 8-minute runtime justifies the one-time block plus the regression risk to any Customer-team query that currently benefits from the region-prefix layout.

When this answer is wrong: If the Customer team's queries depend strongly on region-prefix skipping (e.g., region-filtered queries that run hundreds of times per day), re-Z-ORDERing would regress those queries by the inverse of the audit improvement. The right answer in that case is option D's liquid clustering migration — a larger architectural investment that benefits both query patterns.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level remedy is a 'Z-ORDER effectiveness' dashboard that compares each table's Z-ORDER columns against the actual query patterns over the last quarter. For each Z-ORDERed table, it reports the percentage of queries that match the Z-ORDER's prefix structure — values below 50% trigger a layout review at the next quarterly OPTIMIZE planning meeting.

Deferred short answer (Q4): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The Analytics team has the option to re-Z-ORDER on (country, date, region) at the next quarterly OPTIMIZE. The Customer team's per-customer queries currently benefit from the (country, region, date) layout's region-prefix skipping. What single piece of evidence should the Analytics team gather before proposing the re-Z-ORDER?
→ Query the cluster's query history (or Unity Catalog's query log if available) for the last 30 days, filter to queries on this archive table, and count what fraction filter on region as a prefix. If the fraction is small (under 10%), the regression risk is low and the re-Z-ORDER is likely the right call. If the fraction is high (say, 40%+), the team should compare the audit-query gain against the Customer-team regression and consider liquid clustering instead.

tasks.pssReference: PSS7 lesson 5 (Delta Lake Internals — Z-ORDER and OPTIMIZE), PSS7 lesson 3 (Predicate Evaluation in the Reader — file-level statistics), PSS3 lessons 4-5 (Partition Strategy — when partition columns vs Z-ORDER columns are appropriate).

tasks.title: Five-Join Pipeline Without a Materialization Point

description.context: GridUnion Continental, the cross-country regulatory aggregation pipeline owned by the Platform team. Reads meters → joins to substations → joins to customers → joins to tariffs → joins to country_metadata → joins to regulatory_classifications → aggregates by (country, customer_type, regulatory_class). All five joins are in a single Spark application; the pipeline writes Gold output partitioned by country and date. Current runtime is 85 minutes on the production cluster. The team has been asked to bring this under 60 minutes for the BNetzA quarterly audit window. AQE is enabled, autoBroadcastJoinThreshold is 10MB.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — the team_context block names the operational pressures in play — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, count, broadcast

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName("cross_country_regulatory_aggregate")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
    .getOrCreate())

# 12M readings across all four countries (the 8TB historical archive)
meters = spark.read.table("silver.meters")

# Reference tables — sizes in comments for reviewer context
substations = spark.read.table("silver.substations")              # ~1,800 rows
customers = spark.read.table("silver.customers")                   # 3.5M rows, ~280 MB
tariffs = spark.read.table("silver.tariffs")                       # ~200 rows
country_metadata = spark.read.table("silver.country_metadata")     # 4 rows
regulatory_classifications = spark.read.table("silver.regulatory_classifications")  # ~80 rows

# Five-join chain — the topology this exercise asks about
result = (meters
