    .join(substations.select("substation_id", "region", "capacity_mw"),
          on="substation_id", how="inner")
    .join(customers.select("customer_id", "customer_type", "country"),
          on="customer_id", how="inner")
    .join(tariffs.select("tariff_code", "price_eur_per_kwh"),
          on="tariff_code", how="inner")
    .join(country_metadata.select("country", "timezone", "regulator_id"),
          on="country", how="inner")
    .join(regulatory_classifications.select("customer_type", "regulator_id", "regulatory_class"),
          on=["customer_type", "regulator_id"], how="inner")
    .groupBy("country", "customer_type", "regulatory_class", "date")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"),
        avg("price_eur_per_kwh").alias("avg_price"),
        count("*").alias("reading_count")
    ))

result.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("country", "date").parquet("gold/cross_country_regulatory/")

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: The pipeline is owned by the Platform team and serves four downstream consumers: the BNetzA quarterly audit (DE), the CRE quarterly audit (FR), the ACM quarterly audit (NL), and the ILR quarterly audit (LU). The Platform team has historically resisted introducing intermediate Gold tables in this pipeline because each intermediate table requires its own owner, schema, freshness SLA, and maintenance schedule. The Platform team is small (5 engineers across all of GridUnion's platform infrastructure) and views every new table as a future on-call obligation. The audit window for all four regulators is the same quarterly date — meaning all four audits run simultaneously, intensifying the runtime concern.

fields.label: Q1 — Of the five joins in this pipeline, which is the heaviest at runtime?
options: The first join (meters to substations) — it processes the full 12M-row meters input before any filtering, which makes it the largest data volume any operator in this pipeline ever sees.
The second join (meters-enriched to customers) — customers is 280 MB and exceeds the 10 MB broadcast threshold, so it runs as a SortMergeJoin and is the only non-broadcastable join in the chain.

The fifth join (to regulatory_classifications) — it has the largest output cardinality due to the regulatory_class fan-out, which multiplies output rows by the number of classifications per customer_type.

The fourth join (to country_metadata) — even though country_metadata is 4 rows, the join key 'country' has very low cardinality and produces extreme partition skew that dominates the wall-clock runtime.
fields.correctAnswer: The second join (meters-enriched to customers) — customers is 280 MB and exceeds the 10 MB broadcast threshold, so it runs as a SortMergeJoin and is the only non-broadcastable join in the chain.
fields.rationale:
Sizing the joins: substations (1,800 rows, well under 10MB → BroadcastHashJoin), customers (3.5M rows / 280MB → SortMergeJoin, the only non-broadcastable join), tariffs (200 rows → broadcast), country_metadata (4 rows → broadcast), regulatory_classifications (80 rows → broadcast). The customers join is the only one that requires a full shuffle on the 12M-row probe side, and SMJ shuffle is dramatically more expensive than the broadcast joins around it. Identifying which join is the bottleneck is the first diagnostic step a senior engineer takes when reading a multi-hop pipeline — broadcast cost is roughly free, SMJ shuffle cost is what dominates the runtime budget.

When this answer is wrong: If the cluster's autoBroadcastJoinThreshold were configured higher (e.g., 500MB), the customers table would also broadcast and the diagnosis shifts. With the standard 10MB threshold, the 280MB customers table is unambiguously the bottleneck.

fields.label: Q2 — Where in the join chain is the natural materialization boundary — the point where introducing an intermediate Gold write would have the largest runtime benefit?
options: Before the customers join (after substations) — materializing here gives the customers SortMergeJoin a smaller probe-side input, since the substations enrichment has reduced row count slightly via inner-join semantics.

After the customers join — once customers has been joined and the customer_type / country columns are present, downstream joins are all small-broadcast and the data volume is at its maximum, so materializing here lets re-runs skip the most expensive shuffle.

Before the regulatory_classifications join — materializing here lets the join run in isolation with its own bucket layout chosen specifically for the regulatory_class fan-out, eliminating the skew that the fifth join introduces.

After the country_metadata join — country_metadata adds the regulator_id column that the regulatory_classifications join requires; materializing here breaks the chain at the precise point where the data first contains all columns needed for the final aggregate.
fields.correctAnswer: After the customers join — once customers has been joined and the customer_type / country columns are present, downstream joins are all small-broadcast and the data volume is at its maximum, so materializing here lets re-runs skip the most expensive shuffle.
fields.rationale:
After the customers join, the pipeline has paid the only expensive (SortMergeJoin) cost in the chain. Joins 3, 4, and 5 are all broadcasts of small reference tables; their cumulative cost is small relative to the customers join. Materializing AFTER the customers join lets any re-run, error recovery, or alternate downstream consumer pick up the post-customer-join enriched data without re-paying the SMJ. The materialization point is where the data is largest (after enrichment, before regulatory filtering reduces it) and where the upstream cost is concentrated. The 'natural boundary' is determined by where the cost discontinuity lies, not by where the schema is most convenient.

When this answer is wrong: If a downstream consumer specifically needed only the pre-customer-join data (rare), boundary A would be correct. The audit's actual consumer needs the customer_type column, so the boundary must be after that column is added — which the customers join provides.

fields.label: Q3 — What does materializing at the chosen boundary cost the Platform team operationally, beyond the runtime improvement to this single pipeline?
options: An additional Gold table requires its own owner, schema declaration, freshness SLA, lineage entry, monitoring rule, and quarterly maintenance — all of which the small Platform team must absorb on top of their existing 200+ table inventory.

An additional Gold table doubles the storage footprint of the cross-country pipeline at GridUnion's archival rates, adding roughly €40,000 annually in cloud storage costs that must be reallocated across the four-country chargeback model.

An additional Gold table breaks the existing lineage tooling because it introduces a level of indirection that GridUnion's internal lineage tracker (last upgraded in 2024) does not handle correctly for partitioned multi-country writes.

An additional Gold table requires the Platform team to coordinate with each of the four country-specific regulators on the new table's audit-trail format, which historically takes 6 to 9 months per regulator.
fields.correctAnswer: An additional Gold table requires its own owner, schema declaration, freshness SLA, lineage entry, monitoring rule, and quarterly maintenance — all of which the small Platform team must absorb on top of their existing 200+ table inventory.
fields.rationale:
The senior trade-off is operational, not technical. A new Gold table is a future on-call burden — the Platform team must own its schema (with all the schema-evolution implications from PSS5), declare its freshness SLA (PSS10 lesson 4), and maintain its lineage entry (PSS10 lesson 1). At GridUnion's scale (200+ tables, 5 platform engineers), each new table consumes roughly 1-2% of the team's annual maintenance budget. The Platform team's reluctance to introduce intermediate tables is correct in principle; this exercise tests whether the engineer can balance the runtime benefit against the operational cost rather than treating tables as free.

When this answer is wrong: On a larger platform team (15+ engineers) the per-table operational cost is proportionally smaller and option A's framing as a binding constraint becomes weaker. On a 5-engineer team, the constraint is real and dominates.

fields.label: Q4 — The Platform team's lead proposes an alternative: keep the single pipeline, but refactor it to apply the country filter for each audit at the start (run four separate single-country pipelines instead of one cross-country pipeline). Why is this the WRONG architectural choice for this scenario?
options: Four single-country pipelines run in parallel during the quarterly audit window and saturate the cluster's compute budget; the cross-country pipeline shares its compute footprint across countries via partial aggregation, which the per-country split eliminates entirely.

The bulk of the pipeline's expense is the customers join, and each of the four single-country pipelines would still pay that cost in full; the proposal multiplies the dominant cost by four without addressing the bottleneck that the materialization boundary directly attacks.

The four single-country pipelines each produce their own Gold table per country, which the BNetzA audit specifically rejects — the audit requires a single cross-country table for cross-border benchmarking that the current pipeline's structure makes available as a side effect.

Single-country pipelines defeat AQE's coalescePartitions adaptation because each pipeline's data volume is below the threshold at which AQE engages; the cross-country pipeline's larger volume ensures AQE participates in the optimization.
fields.correctAnswer: The bulk of the pipeline's expense is the customers join, and each of the four single-country pipelines would still pay that cost in full; the proposal multiplies the dominant cost by four without addressing the bottleneck that the materialization boundary directly attacks.
fields.rationale:
Splitting into four single-country pipelines does not address the bottleneck. The customers SortMergeJoin runs once per pipeline; with the cross-country pipeline it runs once over 12M rows; with the four split pipelines it runs four times over ~3M rows each. The total work increases (each pipeline reads its own slice of the substations and meters tables, even though they overlap on shared dimensions). The lead's instinct that 'simpler is better' is the right instinct for code complexity but the wrong instinct for runtime topology — the cross-country structure is exactly the structure that lets the customers join scale by allowing the whole pipeline to amortize its cost across countries.

When this answer is wrong: If the cluster were severely memory-constrained and could not handle a 12M-row SMJ at all, splitting into per-country pipelines might be the only feasible approach despite the inefficiency. The cluster has the capacity here; the architectural problem is runtime, not capacity.

fields.label: Q5 — The BNetzA audit window is 8 weeks away. Given the Platform team's small size and the audit deadline, what is the senior-correct sequencing of fixes — the smallest architectural change that meets the deadline, with the larger change scheduled for after?
options: Now: introduce the materialization boundary as proposed in Q2, accepting the new Gold table's operational cost as the one-time price of meeting the audit deadline. Later: nothing further; the materialization is the architectural endpoint and no follow-up work is needed beyond routine maintenance.

Now: enable AQE's skewJoin and tighten advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes to bring the runtime under 60 minutes without any new tables. Later (next quarter): introduce the materialization boundary once the small Platform team has hired the additional engineer the table inventory growth justifies.

Now: introduce a temporary intermediate write that is owned by the audit pipeline itself (no new shared table; output is written to a /tmp path the audit owns and cleans up). Later: convert to a shared Gold table once a second consumer demonstrates value, justifying the operational cost.

Now: split into four single-country pipelines as the lead proposed; this is operationally simpler to reason about even if not strictly the optimal performance fix. Later: revisit the cross-country join once the team has bandwidth for a deeper architectural review of the topology.
fields.correctAnswer: Now: introduce a temporary intermediate write that is owned by the audit pipeline itself (no new shared table; output is written to a /tmp path the audit owns and cleans up). Later: convert to a shared Gold table once a second consumer demonstrates value, justifying the operational cost.
fields.rationale:
The temporary intermediate write is the senior pattern when you face an architectural cost (new shared Gold table, with all its ownership burden) but a near-term deadline that does not give time to do the larger architectural change correctly. The audit pipeline owns its own intermediate output, writes it to a path the audit pipeline manages, cleans it up after the audit completes. There is no new shared table for the platform team to own. If a second consumer later demonstrates value, the conversation shifts to making it a shared Gold table — at that point the operational cost is amortized across two consumers and easier to justify. This is the 'rule of three' in API design (do not abstract until you have three concrete cases) applied to data-platform architecture.

When this answer is wrong: If the cluster's runtime regressed to where AQE tuning alone could meet the deadline (option B), the smaller fix would be appropriate. The runtime gap (85 → 60 minutes is a 30% reduction) is too large for AQE tuning alone to close reliably.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level pattern that makes materialization decisions repeatable is a 'multi-hop runtime audit' that runs quarterly across all multi-join pipelines. For each pipeline, it identifies which join is the bottleneck (largest non-broadcast join), measures the runtime contribution of each segment, and flags pipelines where introducing a materialization boundary would save more than 30% runtime. The Platform team reviews the flagged candidates with the consuming teams to identify potential second consumers.

Deferred short answer (Q6): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The materialization-boundary decision turns on whether the intermediate result has a second consumer beyond this audit pipeline. Name one likely second consumer at GridUnion that would justify making the materialization a shared Gold table rather than a private intermediate.
→ The Billing team's per-customer invoice pipeline almost certainly needs the same meters+customers+tariff joined view that this audit pipeline materializes; if the Billing team would consume a shared Gold table instead of recomputing the joins themselves, the operational cost of the new table is amortized across at least two consumers. Other plausible second consumers are the Analytics team's customer-segmented dashboards and the ML team's price-prediction features that need the same enrichment.

tasks.pssReference: PSS4 lesson 7 (Multi-Hop Join Optimization — materialization boundaries), PSS4 lesson 1 (Join Topology Analysis), PSS1 lesson 3 (Table Ownership and Access Contracts — operational cost of new tables), PSS10 lesson 4 (Platform SLA Governance).

tasks.title: Join Elimination Should Have Fired But Did Not

description.context: GridUnion Continental, FR deployment. The Analytics team has a query that joins meters to a customers dimension purely as a foreign-key check — the SELECT clause does not project any column from customers. The query was written this way because the original requirement enforced 'no orphaned readings'; the FK-check semantics are now redundant because Bronze ingestion already validates that every reading has a known customer. Catalyst's join-elimination rule should have removed the join entirely (since no column from customers is referenced downstream), but the plan still shows the join. The query takes 11 minutes; eliminating the join would bring it to ~3 minutes.
description.task: Read the physical plan and the per-task metrics, then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true
+- HashAggregate(keys=[region#11, date#15], functions=[sum(daily_kw#16), count(1)])
   +- AQEShuffleRead coalesced
      +- ShuffleQueryStage 3
         +- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#11, date#15, 200)
            +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#11, date#15], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#16), partial_count(1)])
               +- Project [region#11, date#15, daily_kw#16]
                  +- SortMergeJoin [customer_id#14], [customer_id#180], Inner
                     :- Sort [customer_id#14 ASC NULLS FIRST], false, 0
                     :  +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id#14, 200)
                     :     +- FileScan parquet silver_fr.meters [customer_id#14, region#11, date#15, daily_kw#16]
                     +- Sort [customer_id#180 ASC NULLS FIRST], false, 0
                        +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id#180, 200)
                           +- FileScan parquet silver_fr.customers [customer_id#180]

-- Source query --
SELECT region, date, SUM(daily_kw), COUNT(*)
FROM silver_fr.meters m
JOIN silver_fr.customers c ON m.customer_id = c.customer_id
GROUP BY region, date

fields.label: Q1 — Catalyst's join-elimination rule (RemoveRedundantAliases / EliminateOuterJoin family) requires which precondition that this query and these tables do NOT satisfy?
options: Catalyst requires the join to be a LEFT OUTER join with the eliminable side on the right; INNER joins like this one are not eligible for elimination because removing them could change the row count when the right side is missing matching rows.

Catalyst requires the right-side table to declare a primary-key (or unique) constraint on the join column; without the constraint, Catalyst cannot prove the join preserves left-side row count and therefore cannot safely eliminate it from the plan.

Catalyst requires statistics to be refreshed within 24 hours of query execution; the customers table was last analyzed at 02:30 the previous day, putting it just past the freshness window the optimizer uses for elimination decisions on dimension joins.

Catalyst requires the eliminated join's table to be a Delta-format table with column-level statistics; Parquet-format tables are excluded from join elimination because their per-file statistics granularity does not support the rule's correctness check.
fields.correctAnswer: Catalyst requires the right-side table to declare a primary-key (or unique) constraint on the join column; without the constraint, Catalyst cannot prove the join preserves left-side row count and therefore cannot safely eliminate it from the plan.
fields.rationale:
Join elimination is correct only when removing the join cannot change the result. For an INNER join on customer_id, removing the join is safe IFF every left-side row matches exactly one right-side row — which is the case only when customer_id is unique on the right side AND every left-side customer_id has a match (no orphans). Catalyst proves uniqueness from a declared primary-key constraint or a unique constraint on the join column; it does not infer uniqueness from data inspection at planning time. Without the constraint declaration, Catalyst conservatively assumes the join could change row count (right side could have duplicates of customer_id, which would multiply left rows) and leaves the join in place. Option D's NOT NULL on customer_id is necessary but not sufficient — uniqueness is the missing piece.

When this answer is wrong: If the join were a LEFT SEMI JOIN, the elimination semantics differ — a semi-join with NOT NULL plus uniqueness can be eliminated but a semi-join with only NOT NULL cannot. The query here is a regular INNER, so primary-key (uniqueness) is the binding precondition.

fields.label: Q2 — What single metadata-only change to silver_fr.customers would let Catalyst eliminate the join automatically the next time the query runs?
options: Run ANALYZE TABLE silver_fr.customers COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS customer_id; this updates the column-level statistics that Catalyst's elimination rule consults to determine whether customer_id values are unique.

Declare a primary-key constraint on customer_id via ALTER TABLE; Delta does not enforce primary keys at the engine level, but the declaration is recorded in the table metadata and Catalyst's elimination rule reads it directly.

Set the Delta property delta.enableJoinElimination = true on the customers table; the property is off by default and explicitly opts the table in to participating in Catalyst's elimination logic for inner joins.

Add a NOT NULL constraint to customer_id; the column is technically nullable in the metadata even though it has no null values in practice, and Catalyst's elimination requires the join column to be NOT NULL on both sides.
fields.correctAnswer: Declare a primary-key constraint on customer_id via ALTER TABLE; Delta does not enforce primary keys at the engine level, but the declaration is recorded in the table metadata and Catalyst's elimination rule reads it directly.
fields.rationale:
Delta supports declarative primary-key constraints via ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT (or via CREATE TABLE), and Catalyst reads these declarations from the table metadata at planning time. The constraint is informational from a runtime-enforcement perspective (Delta does not reject duplicate-key inserts the way a relational database would), but Catalyst trusts the declaration for optimization decisions. The declaration is the standard architectural pattern for enabling join elimination on Delta tables. Option A (column statistics) gives Catalyst cardinality estimates but not uniqueness proofs. Option C is a fabricated property name. Option D (NOT NULL) is necessary but not sufficient.

When this answer is wrong: On Hive Metastore-backed tables (without Delta), the equivalent is the Spark SQL ALTER TABLE ADD CONSTRAINT syntax, which has been available since Spark 3.4. The mechanism is the same; the platform here uses Unity Catalog with Delta, making option B's pattern correct.

fields.label: Q3 — Suppose the team adds the primary-key declaration. What MUST they verify in the week following the change before assuming join elimination is safe?
options: Verify that no consumer was implicitly relying on the join's row-filtering behavior (e.g., relying on the join to silently drop meter rows whose customer_id has been deleted from customers); a pure FK-check semantics depends on the data quality, not on the join operator.

Verify that the Delta transaction log's checkpoint frequency is at least daily; Catalyst caches the primary-key constraint at planning time and stale cached constraints can produce incorrect query plans during constraint propagation across joins.

Verify that ANALYZE TABLE has been run on every downstream table that joins to silver_fr.customers; constraint declarations propagate through the lineage graph and stale statistics on consumers can produce inconsistent join-elimination decisions across pipelines.

Verify that all four country catalogs (DE, FR, NL, LU) have been updated together; Unity Catalog enforces metadata consistency across catalogs and a constraint declared on FR but not on DE can cause cross-country queries to plan inconsistently between regions.
fields.correctAnswer: Verify that no consumer was implicitly relying on the join's row-filtering behavior (e.g., relying on the join to silently drop meter rows whose customer_id has been deleted from customers); a pure FK-check semantics depends on the data quality, not on the join operator.
fields.rationale:
The architectural risk after declaring the primary-key is not a Catalyst risk — Catalyst will start eliminating the join correctly, given the declared constraint. The risk is that some consumer was implicitly using the join's filtering effect: if a meter row references a customer_id that was deleted from the customers table, the INNER join silently drops that meter row from the result. After elimination, the meter row appears in the aggregate. This is a CHANGE in observed behavior, even though it is the 'correct' behavior given the FK-redundancy reasoning. The team must scan downstream consumers for any that depend on this drop-on-orphan filtering, which is a code review across the consumer pipelines.

When this answer is wrong: If the table is provably free of orphaned references (all meter customer_ids exist in customers — verifiable by a one-time integrity scan), the row-count concern is moot and the change is safe to apply immediately. The team should run that scan first.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level remedy is a 'redundant-join detector' that scans plans across the cluster's query history and flags joins where the joined table contributes no projected columns AND a primary-key declaration on the join key would enable elimination. GridUnion's Platform team runs this detector quarterly and cross-references the results with table-owner teams to identify candidate constraint declarations.

Deferred short answer (Q4): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The Customer team is the owner of silver_fr.customers and is reluctant to declare the primary-key constraint because Delta does not enforce it — they fear that declaring an unenforced constraint creates the appearance of a guarantee the table cannot keep. What is the senior-correct response to this concern?
→ The primary-key declaration is metadata for the optimizer, not a runtime enforcement contract for the table's data quality. The table's actual uniqueness is a separate operational concern, monitored by a duplicate-customer_id detector that the Customer team owns. The declaration tells Catalyst 'you can safely eliminate joins where customer_id appears as a unique key'; it does not promise anything to downstream consumers about data quality.

tasks.pssReference: PSS4 lesson 4 (Join Elimination), PSS5 lesson 4 (Compatibility Rules — constraint declarations as forward-compatible additions), PSS7 lesson 5 (Delta Lake Internals — metadata extensions).

tasks.title: Range Join Across Country Tariff Versions

description.context: GridUnion Continental, monthly per-meter billing pipeline. The pipeline computes each meter's monthly bill by joining meter readings to a slowly-changing tariff dimension (SCD Type 2): each tariff has a customer_type, country, valid_from, and valid_to. The join condition is reading.ts BETWEEN tariff.valid_from AND tariff.valid_to AND reading.customer_type = tariff.customer_type AND reading.country = tariff.country. The DE-only run takes 40 minutes. Replicated naively to all four countries it would run ~160 minutes serial, ~70 minutes parallelized by country. The team is asked to handle the full archive backfill (3 years, ~500M rows, 4 countries) in under 4 hours.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — the team_context block names the operational pressures in play — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, broadcast

spark = (SparkSession.builder
    .appName("monthly_billing")
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
    .getOrCreate())

# Per-country meters; this run is for DE
meters = spark.read.table("silver_de.meters").filter(col("date") >= "2026-04-01")

# Per-country tariff registry (~50 rows per country, SCD Type 2)
# Schema: customer_type, country, tariff_code, price_eur_per_kwh, valid_from, valid_to
tariffs = spark.read.table("silver_de.tariffs")

# The range join — reading.ts must fall within the tariff's validity window
# AND match on customer_type and country
result = (meters.alias("m")
    .join(tariffs.alias("t"),
          (col("m.customer_type") == col("t.customer_type"))
          & (col("m.country") == col("t.country"))
          & (col("m.ts") >= col("t.valid_from"))
          & (col("m.ts") < col("t.valid_to")),
          how="inner")
    .groupBy("m.customer_id", "m.month")
    .agg(sum(col("m.daily_kw") * col("t.price_eur_per_kwh")).alias("monthly_bill_eur")))

result.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("silver_de.monthly_billing/")

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: The Billing team owns the monthly billing pipeline and runs it monthly. The full archive backfill is a quarterly request from the Finance team that re-computes the full three-year history when tariff retroactive corrections occur (~once per quarter, regulatory-driven). Each country has its own tariff registry of approximately 50 active and 100 historical SCD-Type-2 tariff records. The four country tariff registries are stored in separate Silver tables (silver_de.tariffs, silver_fr.tariffs, etc.) but share the same schema. The Finance team's deadline pressure during quarterly retro-corrections is severe — a 4-hour backfill is the operational ceiling before downstream Finance reports begin to slip.

fields.label: Q1 — Why does the standard hash-partitioning strategy that powers most Spark joins not help on this query?
options: Hash partitioning routes rows to executors based on the equi-join key columns; the equi conditions on customer_type and country are too low-cardinality (4 customer_types × 4 countries = 16 distinct keys) to distribute work across the cluster's 200+ executors evenly.

The range condition on ts cannot be expressed as a hash partitioning rule because ranges do not factor into discrete buckets; only equi-join components participate in the shuffle, after which each bucket must perform a Cartesian-style scan of the tariff matches that satisfy the range.

Hash partitioning is deliberately disabled on Spark when one side of the join is below the broadcast threshold; the tariffs table's small size pushes the planner toward a broadcast strategy that bypasses the hash-partition step entirely on every join in the application.

Hash partitioning requires both sides of the join to have matching bucket layouts on the equi-join columns; meters and tariffs are both unbucketed Silver tables, and Catalyst's repartition cost outweighs the benefit on a 50-row tariffs side.
fields.correctAnswer: The range condition on ts cannot be expressed as a hash partitioning rule because ranges do not factor into discrete buckets; only equi-join components participate in the shuffle, after which each bucket must perform a Cartesian-style scan of the tariff matches that satisfy the range.
fields.rationale:
Hash partitioning is fundamentally an equi-join mechanism: rows hash to buckets based on the equi-join key, and bucket-by-bucket the join is performed locally. The range condition (ts BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to) cannot be expressed as a hash; the range crosses bucket boundaries by definition. Spark handles a mixed equi+range condition by hash-partitioning on the equi components only (here, customer_type and country) and then performing a per-bucket nested-loop or interval-merge over the range condition. With low-cardinality equi keys (4×4=16 keys here), most of the work concentrates on a few buckets, which is the runtime bottleneck. This is why range joins do not scale via shuffle the way equi joins do.

When this answer is wrong: On Spark with experimental interval-tree join support (Photon and some 3.5+ branches), the planner can build per-bucket interval indexes that change the cost calculus; this is not GA and not in play at GridUnion.

fields.label: Q2 — The tariffs registry per country is ~50 rows (~5 KB). What architectural pattern resolves this efficiently and what is its name?
options: A bucket-aligned interval merge join — both tables are bucketed on (customer_type, country) and the range condition is evaluated within each bucket via a sorted merge of valid_from / valid_to intervals; this is the standard approach when the dimension exceeds broadcast size.

A broadcast range join — the tiny per-country tariffs table is broadcast to every executor and each executor performs the range match in-memory for its local meters partition; broadcasting eliminates the shuffle and the range condition is evaluated as a simple in-memory loop per partition.

A correlated-subquery rewrite — the join is rewritten as a subquery that selects MAX(valid_from) per (customer_type, country) below reading.ts, removing the explicit range condition by replacing it with an equi-join on a derived column.

An AQE-driven dynamic partition pruning — AQE detects the range predicate at runtime and dynamically partitions the meters scan to read only files whose ts column min/max overlaps with at least one tariff's valid_from / valid_to window.
fields.correctAnswer: A broadcast range join — the tiny per-country tariffs table is broadcast to every executor and each executor performs the range match in-memory for its local meters partition; broadcasting eliminates the shuffle and the range condition is evaluated as a simple in-memory loop per partition.
fields.rationale:
Broadcast range join is the standard architectural pattern when one side of a range join is small enough to broadcast (under autoBroadcastJoinThreshold). Each executor receives the full tariff registry for the relevant country, holds it in memory, and for each local meter row performs a range lookup against the broadcast table. The lookup is O(log n) with a sorted interval structure or O(n) with a linear scan — both are negligible at 50 rows. The shuffle is eliminated; the range evaluation is local and bounded. This is PSS4 lesson 3's canonical recommendation for SCD-Type-2 lookups against small reference dimensions. Option A would apply if tariffs were large; option C is a different rewrite for non-overlapping intervals; option D conflates DPP with range pruning.

When this answer is wrong: If the tariff registry grew beyond broadcast size (say, several MB), bucket-aligned interval merge join (option A) becomes the right pattern. At GridUnion's tariff scale, broadcast remains correct.

fields.label: Q3 — When the Finance team requests the full archive backfill (500M rows × 4 countries), the broadcast range join runs in ~3 hours. The team is asked to bring this further down for the next quarterly retro-correction. What architectural pattern is the next step beyond broadcast range join?
options: Bin-based approximation — divide the tariff timeline into uniform time bins (e.g., daily) and pre-explode each tariff to one row per bin within its validity window; the query then becomes an equi-join on (customer_type, country, bin) and the range condition is gone entirely.

Bucketed range join — re-bucket meters and tariffs on (customer_type, country) with matching bucket counts, so the range condition is evaluated per-bucket without a broadcast; this scales as the dimension grows beyond broadcast size in the future.

Streaming incremental join — convert the batch backfill to a streaming aggregation that processes each day's readings as a micro-batch and accumulates results, reducing peak memory pressure and unlocking parallelism that the batch query cannot exploit.

Materialized intermediate — write the joined-and-priced reading-by-tariff result to an intermediate Gold table once during the quarterly window; subsequent monthly billing runs read the materialized result and avoid re-joining.
fields.correctAnswer: Bin-based approximation — divide the tariff timeline into uniform time bins (e.g., daily) and pre-explode each tariff to one row per bin within its validity window; the query then becomes an equi-join on (customer_type, country, bin) and the range condition is gone entirely.
fields.rationale:
Bin-based approximation is the runtime-bound pattern when even broadcast range join is too slow. The idea: pre-compute, for each tariff, one row per bin within its validity window. With daily bins, a tariff valid for 90 days becomes 90 rows in the binned tariff table. The runtime join is now an equi-join on (customer_type, country, bin) — no range condition, no nested-loop, full hash-partition parallelism. The cost is the one-time bin explosion (which is small for 4 × 50 tariffs × ~3 years of bins) and the storage of the binned tariff table. Option B (bucketed range join) helps as a steady-state pattern but does not collapse the runtime as drastically as eliminating the range condition entirely. Option C (streaming) does not help — backfills are inherently batch. Option D (materialized intermediate) helps for repeat reads but the team is asked to speed up THIS run.

When this answer is wrong: If tariff windows can be very short (e.g., hour-level retroactive corrections), the bin granularity needed becomes fine enough that the bin explosion is impractical; bucketed range join becomes the correct answer. GridUnion's tariffs are typically valid for at least 30 days, making daily bins manageable.

fields.label: Q4 — The bin-based approximation has a correctness caveat. What is it, and how does the team verify the bin choice is safe?
options: Bin granularity must be at least as fine as the smallest tariff validity window in the registry; if any tariff is valid for less than one bin period, that tariff fits inside a bin and the equi-join misses it. Verification: scan the tariffs table for the minimum (valid_to - valid_from) duration before choosing the bin.

Bin granularity must align with Spark's internal timestamp precision; bins finer than 1 millisecond produce floating-point drift in the bin computation that can route the same reading to two bins on consecutive runs of the same query. Verification: bin granularity above 1 millisecond is always safe.

Bin granularity must match the partition column of the meters table; bins coarser than the date partition produce correct results but bins finer than the date partition cause Spark to read multiple files per bin. Verification: align bins to the date partition's granularity (typically daily).

Bin granularity must be a divisor of the tariff registry's update cadence; if tariffs change weekly and bins are 10 days, some bins span two distinct tariff versions. Verification: align bins to the tariff registry's update cadence (typically weekly).
fields.correctAnswer: Bin granularity must be at least as fine as the smallest tariff validity window in the registry; if any tariff is valid for less than one bin period, that tariff fits inside a bin and the equi-join misses it. Verification: scan the tariffs table for the minimum (valid_to - valid_from) duration before choosing the bin.
fields.rationale:
Bin-based approximation is correct ONLY when every tariff window contains at least one full bin period. If a tariff is valid for less than the bin duration (e.g., a 6-hour emergency tariff with daily bins), no bin lies fully inside the window and the bin-equi-join produces no rows for readings in that window — silently dropping correct matches. The verification step is to scan the tariffs table for the minimum window duration (SELECT MIN(valid_to - valid_from) FROM tariffs) and choose a bin period strictly smaller. The other options describe constraints that do not actually exist (B is fabricated; C is operationally relevant but not a correctness constraint; D is only a partial concern).

When this answer is wrong: If the team can guarantee by contract that no tariff will ever be valid for less than 30 days (a regulatory promise the tariff registry maintains), they can fix bin granularity at daily and skip the verification step. Without that contractual guarantee, the verification is mandatory.

Platform-level operational note: The operational rule is to keep the binned tariff table as a transient artifact owned by the backfill pipeline rather than as a Gold table. The pipeline produces it on demand at the start of each backfill and discards it on completion. This avoids the table-ownership burden the Platform team would otherwise inherit and matches the pattern from JAS3.1's 'temporary intermediate' answer.

Deferred short answer (Q5): Short answer (1–2 sentences). The Finance team's quarterly retro-correction is the dominant runtime constraint. The monthly billing run (which is the more frequent workload) is comfortably fast as a broadcast range join. Should the team adopt the bin-based approximation for the monthly run, or only for the quarterly backfill?
→ Only for the backfill. The monthly billing run is already comfortably fast at 40 minutes (DE alone) using broadcast range join — well within the monthly window's tolerance. Introducing the bin-based approximation for the monthly run adds the operational overhead of maintaining a binned tariff table, with no runtime benefit since the existing approach is fast enough. The senior pattern is: optimize the workload that needs optimizing, not the one that already meets its SLA.

tasks.pssReference: PSS4 lesson 3 (Range Joins and Interval Lookups), PSS4 lesson 1 (Join Topology Analysis), PSS3 lesson 7 (Bin-based partition pruning patterns).

tasks.title: Exactly-Once Broken by Sink Configuration

description.context: GridUnion Continental, real-time grid-balance pipeline. Reads from Kafka (topic meter_readings_live, partitioned by region), aggregates per-region power flow on a 5-minute tumbling window, writes to a Delta table that the operations dashboard polls every 30 seconds. Last week the cluster was force-restarted by the platform team during an unrelated maintenance event; on resumption the pipeline ran cleanly with no errors. Two days later the operations team noticed double-counted energy totals for the 14:00-14:05 window — every row that landed in the Delta table during the window straddling the restart was duplicated. The streaming pipeline owner insists checkpoints are configured correctly. The Delta table owner insists the table is configured correctly. Both are individually right; the system is wrong.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — the team_context block names the operational pressures in play — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, window, expr
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, TimestampType

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("grid_balance_realtime").getOrCreate()

schema = StructType([
    StructField("meter_id", StringType(), False),
    StructField("region", StringType(), False),
    StructField("country", StringType(), False),
    StructField("daily_kw", DoubleType(), False),
    StructField("event_ts", TimestampType(), False)
])

# Source: Kafka, 32 partitions, 1 partition per region
readings = (spark.readStream
    .format("kafka")
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "kafka.gridunion.internal:9093")
    .option("subscribe", "meter_readings_live")
    .option("startingOffsets", "earliest")
    .load()
    .select(expr("CAST(value AS STRING)").alias("json"))
    .select(expr("from_json(json, 'meter_id STRING, region STRING, country STRING, daily_kw DOUBLE, event_ts TIMESTAMP')").alias("r"))
    .select("r.*"))

# 5-minute tumbling window aggregation
windowed = (readings
    .withWatermark("event_ts", "15 minutes")
    .groupBy(window(col("event_ts"), "5 minutes"), col("region"))
    .agg(sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw")))

# Sink: Delta table, append mode, dashboard polls this table
query = (windowed.writeStream
    .format("delta")
    .outputMode("append")
    .option("checkpointLocation", "/checkpoints/grid_balance_realtime/")
    .trigger(processingTime="1 minute")
    .start("/silver_de/grid_balance_5min/"))

query.awaitTermination()

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: The Streaming team owns the pipeline and points to the checkpointLocation as evidence that exactly-once is configured. The Delta team owns the sink table. The Delta team's runbook states 'Delta sinks are exactly-once when written from a Spark streaming query that uses idempotent commits'. Neither team has read the other team's runbook. The cluster restart during maintenance interrupted a micro-batch that had partially written its output files to the Delta table directory but had not yet committed the Delta transaction. On restart, the streaming query resumed from its last checkpointed Kafka offset and re-processed the same micro-batch.

fields.label: Q1 — What did the cluster restart leave in an inconsistent state, and what did the streaming query do on resumption that produced the duplicates?
options: The restart killed the Spark driver mid-checkpoint, leaving the Kafka offsets ahead of the actual processed state; on resumption the query skipped the unprocessed micro-batch and produced underseen rows rather than duplicates.

The restart interrupted a micro-batch after data files were written to the Delta directory but before the Delta transaction commit ran; on resumption the query re-read the same Kafka offsets and re-wrote the batch, leaving the original orphan files in place plus the new committed files.

The restart corrupted the checkpoint directory's RocksDB state store, causing the streaming query to lose its watermark state on resumption; the missing watermark caused late-arriving readings to be re-aggregated alongside their original aggregation results.

The restart broke the Kafka consumer group lease, causing Kafka to rebalance partitions across executors mid-stream; the rebalance produced duplicate messages because some partitions were consumed twice during the handover window.
fields.correctAnswer: The restart interrupted a micro-batch after data files were written to the Delta directory but before the Delta transaction commit ran; on resumption the query re-read the same Kafka offsets and re-wrote the batch, leaving the original orphan files in place plus the new committed files.
fields.rationale:
Spark streaming with Delta sink writes a micro-batch as a two-step process: first the data files are written to the Delta table directory, then a transaction log entry is committed that points to those files. The restart killed the driver between these two steps, leaving data files on disk but no transaction entry pointing to them — these are 'orphan files' from Delta's perspective and are invisible to readers. On resumption, the streaming query reads its checkpoint, sees that the last micro-batch did not complete (no commit recorded), and re-processes the same Kafka offsets. The re-processed batch writes a NEW set of data files AND commits the transaction. Delta now has two physical sets of data files but only one is committed and visible to readers — except in this scenario the original orphan files were also picked up by a subsequent reader path, producing duplicates. The two-phase commit boundary is the fault line.

When this answer is wrong: If the checkpoint directory had also been corrupted, the failure mode would shift toward option C; this scenario states that the checkpoint resumed cleanly, so the corruption case is excluded.

fields.label: Q2 — The Streaming team is correct that checkpoints are configured. The Delta team is correct that Delta supports exactly-once writes. Why does the system still produce duplicates?
options: Delta exactly-once requires the streaming write to declare a transactional identifier (txnAppId and txnVersion) so that Delta can recognize a re-attempted micro-batch and skip it; the snippet does not declare these options, so the re-attempted batch on resumption appears to Delta as a new write.

Delta exactly-once requires the streaming write to use outputMode 'complete' rather than 'append'; complete mode rewrites the full aggregate state on each micro-batch, which makes duplicates impossible because each commit overwrites the previous state.

Delta exactly-once requires the checkpoint location to be on the same storage account as the Delta table; cross-storage-account checkpoints lose transactional context between the checkpoint write and the Delta commit and cannot guarantee consistency on restart.

Delta exactly-once requires a periodic OPTIMIZE that compacts orphaned files; without an OPTIMIZE schedule the orphan files from interrupted commits accumulate and are read by readers as if they were committed data, producing the appearance of duplicates.
fields.correctAnswer: Delta exactly-once requires the streaming write to declare a transactional identifier (txnAppId and txnVersion) so that Delta can recognize a re-attempted micro-batch and skip it; the snippet does not declare these options, so the re-attempted batch on resumption appears to Delta as a new write.
fields.rationale:
Delta supports exactly-once for streaming writes via the transactional commit pattern, but the writer must opt in by declaring txnAppId (a stable identifier for the streaming application) and txnVersion (a per-batch monotonic identifier). With these declared, the Delta commit protocol records them in the transaction log; on restart, before committing a micro-batch, Delta checks whether that (appId, version) pair has already been committed and skips the commit if so — treating the re-processed batch as idempotent. Without the declaration, Delta has no way to recognize that a re-attempted batch is the same as a previously-committed one; each micro-batch commit appears to Delta as a fresh write. Both teams' runbooks are individually correct: checkpoints DO recover the offsets, and Delta DOES support exactly-once — but only when the writer participates in the protocol via txnAppId / txnVersion.

When this answer is wrong: If the application were using foreachBatch with a custom commit function, the txnAppId / txnVersion mechanism would not apply — the application owner is responsible for the dedup logic in that case. The snippet here uses the default streaming-Delta sink, where txnAppId / txnVersion is the prescribed pattern.

fields.label: Q3 — How does Delta's transactional sink use txnAppId and txnVersion to achieve exactly-once on a streaming write?
options: Each micro-batch declares (txnAppId, txnVersion) in its commit metadata; on restart, before starting a micro-batch, the writer checks the Delta log for that pair and skips the micro-batch if it has already committed, treating the duplicate write as a no-op.

The Delta protocol uses txnAppId to assign an executor-local sequence number to each row written, so duplicate rows from re-processed micro-batches are detected at row-level via the sequence and deduplicated during the next OPTIMIZE pass.

The txnVersion is propagated to the Spark UI as a per-batch identifier, allowing the streaming team to manually identify and trigger compensating writes for any micro-batch that committed twice; the dedup itself is operational, not automatic.

The txnAppId is hashed into the file paths Delta writes during a streaming commit; identical hashes from re-processed batches produce path collisions that the storage layer rejects with a write error, preventing the duplicate from being created.
fields.correctAnswer: Each micro-batch declares (txnAppId, txnVersion) in its commit metadata; on restart, before starting a micro-batch, the writer checks the Delta log for that pair and skips the micro-batch if it has already committed, treating the duplicate write as a no-op.
fields.rationale:
The Delta protocol's exactly-once mechanism is a commit-metadata check: each micro-batch's commit includes (txnAppId, txnVersion) in the log entry. On restart, before processing a micro-batch, the writer looks up the (appId, version) pair in the log; if it is already present, the writer concludes that the batch has already committed and skips it as a no-op. This is the same pattern as a database transaction's log-based recovery. Option B (per-row sequencing) is not how it works — Delta deduplicates at batch granularity, not row granularity. Option C (UI-based detection) is wrong; the dedup is automatic. Option D (path collision) is fabricated.

When this answer is wrong: On Delta with experimental row-level concurrency features, the per-row mechanism in option B becomes partially relevant for non-streaming concurrent writes; this is not the streaming exactly-once mechanism even on those configurations.

fields.label: Q4 — Identify the architectural fault that produced this incident — the fault that the (txnAppId, txnVersion) configuration is the symptomatic remedy for. The fault is upstream of the configuration line.
options: The Streaming team and the Delta team operate without a shared contract for exactly-once that names which side declares which configuration; each team's runbook claims exactly-once without specifying the cross-team configuration that achieves it together.

The cluster's maintenance procedure does not gracefully drain streaming queries before force-restart; without the drain, every streaming query is exposed to the same partial-commit risk regardless of how the sink is configured.

The Kafka topic's partition layout (32 partitions, 1 per region) puts the entire DE region on a single Kafka partition; on restart, the single-partition design serializes the recovery and amplifies any duplication that occurs.

The dashboard polls the Delta table every 30 seconds, which is more aggressive than the streaming write trigger of 1 minute; the polling cadence creates window-boundary observation effects that surface as visible 'duplicates' even when the underlying data is correct.
fields.correctAnswer: The Streaming team and the Delta team operate without a shared contract for exactly-once that names which side declares which configuration; each team's runbook claims exactly-once without specifying the cross-team configuration that achieves it together.
fields.rationale:
The architectural fault is the absence of a shared contract between the source-side team (Streaming) and the sink-side team (Delta) that names which side declares which configuration to achieve the cross-team property both teams claim. Each team's runbook is internally consistent: Streaming says 'checkpoints give us source-side exactly-once', Delta says 'transactional commits give us sink-side exactly-once'. Neither runbook says 'and the streaming write must declare txnAppId/txnVersion for these to compose into end-to-end exactly-once'. The configuration line is the symptomatic remedy; the fault is the contract gap. The same gap will produce different incidents in different cross-team integrations until the contract is written. Option B describes a real concern (the lack of graceful drain), but graceful drain is a mitigation against the same underlying gap, not a different fault.

When this answer is wrong: On a single-team-owned pipeline (where one engineer owns both the source and the sink), the contract gap is internal and option A is misframed. GridUnion's two-team ownership makes the gap external and contractual, which is what option A names.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level remedy is a deployment-gate review for streaming pipelines that requires a signed exactly-once contract before the pipeline can deploy to production. The contract names the source format, the sink format, the checkpoint configuration, and any sink-specific opt-in (such as txnAppId / txnVersion for Delta sinks). Both source and sink team leads sign; the Platform team archives the signed contract in the pipeline's lineage entry.

Deferred short answer (Q5): Short answer (1–2 sentences). What single artifact at the platform level would have prevented this incident — not a configuration change, but an organizational mechanism that catches this class of cross-team gap before production?
→ A platform-owned 'streaming exactly-once contract' document that lists, for each supported source-sink combination, the configuration each side must declare for end-to-end exactly-once. The document is a checklist signed off by both the source team and the sink team at deployment review; the Platform team owns the document and updates it as new sources or sinks are added.

tasks.pssReference: PSS9 lesson 5 (Exactly-Once Semantics in Spark Streaming — sink-specific opt-ins), PSS9 lesson 2 (Checkpoint Recovery and the Two-Phase Commit Boundary), PSS5 lesson 3 (Data Contracts — cross-team configuration as contract), PSS10 lesson 5 (Incident Management — same-class incident prevention).

tasks.title: Watermark vs State Cleanup Race

description.context: GridUnion Continental, the FR Analytics team's hourly aggregation streaming pipeline. The query computes per-region average price per kWh on a 1-hour tumbling window with a 1-hour watermark. The pipeline has been running for 18 hours since deployment yesterday morning. The state store size has grown monotonically over those 18 hours, from 200 MB at startup to 14 GB now. The Streaming team expected the state to stabilize around 800 MB once the watermark rotated through the first hour's worth of windows. The driver is reporting GC pauses every micro-batch and the latest commit took 47 seconds.
description.task: Read the physical plan and the per-task metrics, then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: df.explain() — physical plan
evidence.content:
== Physical Plan ==
WriteToDataSourceV2 DeltaSource
+- StateStoreSave [region#11, hour_of_day#117, window#88], state info [ checkpoint = checkpoints/fr_hourly_avg, runId = 7d3a..., opId = 0, ver = 412, numPartitions = 200 ], Append, 1745234890000, 0, 2
   +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#11, hour_of_day#117, window#88], functions=[merge_avg(price_eur_per_kwh#88)])
      +- StateStoreRestore [region#11, hour_of_day#117, window#88], state info [ checkpoint = checkpoints/fr_hourly_avg, runId = 7d3a..., opId = 0, ver = 412, numPartitions = 200 ], 2
         +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#11, hour_of_day#117, window#88], functions=[partial_avg(price_eur_per_kwh#88)])
            +- EventTimeWatermark event_ts#15: timestamp, interval 1 hours
               +- Project [region#11, hour(event_ts#15) AS hour_of_day#117, window(event_ts#15, 1 hour) AS window#88, price_eur_per_kwh#88, event_ts#15]
                  +- StreamingRelation kafka, [meter_id, region, country, price_eur_per_kwh, event_ts]

-- Source query --
readings
  .withWatermark("event_ts", "1 hour")
  .withColumn("hour_of_day", hour(col("event_ts")))
  .groupBy(
     window(col("event_ts"), "1 hour"),
     col("region"),
     col("hour_of_day"))
  .agg(avg("price_eur_per_kwh").alias("avg_price"))

fields.label: Q1 — The watermark is rotating correctly (it advances by ~1 hour per hour of wall-clock time). State rows added per micro-batch is 720,000 but state rows removed is zero. Why does the state never shrink?
options: The state store's RocksDB compaction is misconfigured for the cluster's disk profile, so removed rows accumulate as tombstones rather than being physically deleted from the state file; the logical state is correct but the physical size grows monotonically until a forced compaction runs.

The grouping key includes hour_of_day, derived from event_ts via the hour() function; hour_of_day cycles 0-23 independently of event_ts, so the watermark cannot age out a (region, hour_of_day, window) tuple because the same (region, hour_of_day) reappears every 24 hours and the state retains the running aggregate.

The watermark interval (1 hour) equals the window size (1 hour), which Spark interprets as 'no late data tolerated' and disables state cleanup entirely as a precaution; widening the watermark to 1 hour 5 minutes would re-enable cleanup and allow state to stabilize at the expected 800 MB.

Append mode does not emit final aggregate rows until the window has fully closed past the watermark; the rows are computed but held in state pending emission, and on append mode the state grows for the duration of the watermark interval before any emission triggers cleanup.
fields.correctAnswer: The grouping key includes hour_of_day, derived from event_ts via the hour() function; hour_of_day cycles 0-23 independently of event_ts, so the watermark cannot age out a (region, hour_of_day, window) tuple because the same (region, hour_of_day) reappears every 24 hours and the state retains the running aggregate.
fields.rationale:
The watermark mechanism evicts state rows whose grouping key contains a window column that has aged past the watermark. Here the grouping key is (region, hour_of_day, window) — three columns. The window column ages out cleanly: a window with upper bound 11:00 is past a watermark of 12:00 (assuming the 1-hour watermark) and the (region, hour_of_day, window=11:00) row is eligible for cleanup. But the state stores one row per UNIQUE grouping key tuple, and as time advances NEW grouping keys appear: (region, hour_of_day, window=12:00), (region, hour_of_day, window=13:00), etc. The hour_of_day part of the key is independent of event_ts in the long run, so for each region+hour_of_day combination, every successive window adds a new state row. The watermark removes old (region, hour_of_day, window) tuples eventually, but new ones are added at the same or faster rate. The state grows monotonically until it stabilizes at (number of regions × 24 hours × number of unfinished windows in flight). The 'state never shrinks' observation is technically wrong — old rows DO age out — but new rows are added faster.

When this answer is wrong: Option D's diagnosis (append mode delays emission) describes a real Spark behavior but is not the cause here; emission delay would produce a temporary state hump, not the unbounded growth observed. The cause is the grouping-key composition.

fields.label: Q2 — What does Spark's structured streaming use to determine when a stateful aggregation row is eligible for cleanup?
options: Spark uses the current watermark value compared against each grouping key's most recent event_ts; rows whose most recent event_ts is older than the watermark are eligible for removal regardless of the grouping key composition.

Spark uses the current watermark value compared against the upper bound of the window column embedded in each grouping key; rows whose window upper bound is older than the watermark are eligible for removal, which requires the window to be present as a grouping key.

Spark uses a state-row TTL set explicitly via the spark.sql.streaming.stateStore.maintenanceInterval property; rows older than the TTL are removed during the next maintenance pass independent of the watermark.

Spark uses the order of micro-batch arrivals to estimate event-time progression; rows that have not been touched in the last N micro-batches are eligible for cleanup, where N is the watermark interval divided by the trigger interval.
fields.correctAnswer: Spark uses the current watermark value compared against the upper bound of the window column embedded in each grouping key; rows whose window upper bound is older than the watermark are eligible for removal, which requires the window to be present as a grouping key.
fields.rationale:
Spark's streaming state cleanup is window-bound, not event_ts-bound. The cleanup criterion is: a state row is eligible for removal when the window column embedded in its grouping key has its upper bound older than the current watermark. This means (a) the window column must be present in the grouping key for ANY cleanup to occur, and (b) cleanup is per-key — different keys age out independently as their respective windows close. Option A is wrong because event_ts is not stored per grouping key in the state, only the window is. Option C describes a property that does not exist in this form. Option D is fabricated.

When this answer is wrong: On Spark with newer state APIs (FlatMapGroupsWithState, TransformWithState), the cleanup can be driven by application-defined logic rather than the window-bound rule; this scenario uses the standard streaming aggregation API where option B is the cleanup mechanism.

fields.label: Q3 — Given the actual cleanup mechanism (option B above), why does the hour_of_day column being part of the grouping key cause the state to grow without bound?
options: hour_of_day cycles 0-23 and is independent of event_ts in the long run; for any given (region, hour_of_day, window) tuple the window column eventually ages out of the watermark, but a NEW tuple for the same (region, hour_of_day) appears in a future window and is treated as a fresh state row.

hour_of_day is a derived column that Spark cannot trace back to event_ts in the watermark dependency graph; the watermark applies to event_ts but does not propagate to hour_of_day, so any grouping that includes hour_of_day is treated as ineligible for cleanup.

hour_of_day produces 24 distinct values, multiplying the cardinality of the grouping key 24× compared to a (region, window) grouping; the cleanup runs at the same rate but the state grows 24× faster than it shrinks during the warm-up period.

hour_of_day is a string column in the plan even though it represents an integer; Spark's state cleanup defaults to keeping string-typed grouping columns that have a high distinct count, and the keys here exceed the threshold that disables automatic eviction.
fields.correctAnswer: hour_of_day cycles 0-23 and is independent of event_ts in the long run; for any given (region, hour_of_day, window) tuple the window column eventually ages out of the watermark, but a NEW tuple for the same (region, hour_of_day) appears in a future window and is treated as a fresh state row.
fields.rationale:
The window column ages out correctly, but for each (region, hour_of_day) pair, every new window introduces a new state row. Over 18 hours of operation: 4 regions × 24 hours of day × 18 windows accumulated = 1,728 distinct (region, hour_of_day, window) tuples per meter, multiplied by the meters that have reported data within each tuple's window. The state row count of 12.48M visible in the metrics is consistent with this calculation. The watermark IS aging out old windows; the addition of new (region, hour_of_day, window=N+1) tuples for each elapsed hour outpaces removal during the warm-up phase. The pattern stabilizes only once every region+hour_of_day combination has its full window-cycle of state rows in flight, at roughly 4 × 24 × N rows where N is the watermark interval expressed in window units. With more granular keys (here meter-level data per region) this stable state size is enormous.

When this answer is wrong: If hour_of_day were a column whose values were bounded by a small integer range that did not appear in successive windows (e.g., 'first three readings of the day'), the unbounded growth would not occur and option C's cardinality framing would be the right way to describe a fixable transient hump.

fields.label: Q4 — What is the architectural fix here? The query intent is 'per-region average price by hour-of-day, on rolling 1-hour windows' — the team wants to keep this analytical view.
options: Move the hour_of_day grouping out of the streaming aggregation and into a downstream batch query that reads the streaming output; the streaming pipeline groups by (region, window) only and a downstream query buckets by hour_of_day after the fact.

Replace the watermark interval from 1 hour to 1 day; the wider interval gives Spark enough room to age out the (region, hour_of_day, window) tuples on a daily basis rather than on an hourly basis, bounding the state at a 24-hour rolling footprint.

Set spark.sql.streaming.stateStore.providerClass to RocksDBStateStoreProvider to scale state to disk; the state will still grow but disk-backed state does not produce GC pauses on the driver and the pipeline runs stably at any state size.

Add an additional withWatermark on a derived column hour_window=date_trunc('hour', event_ts) and group by hour_window; the additional watermark gives Spark a second cleanup criterion that propagates through the derived hour_of_day column.
fields.correctAnswer: Move the hour_of_day grouping out of the streaming aggregation and into a downstream batch query that reads the streaming output; the streaming pipeline groups by (region, window) only and a downstream query buckets by hour_of_day after the fact.
fields.rationale:
The fix is to remove hour_of_day from the streaming grouping key — the streaming pipeline groups by (region, window) only, which is a finite-state aggregation that the watermark properly bounds. A downstream batch query reads the streaming output and groups by hour_of_day after the fact, taking the per-window averages and re-aggregating them into per-hour-of-day buckets. This separates the streaming concern (incremental aggregation with bounded state) from the analytical concern (post-hoc bucketing for the dashboard view). Option B (wider watermark) makes the problem larger, not smaller. Option C (RocksDB) just hides the symptom. Option D (extra watermark on a derived column) is a fabricated mechanism — Spark only tracks one watermark per query.

When this answer is wrong: If the dashboard requires sub-second freshness on the hour-of-day breakdown (rare), the post-hoc batch query would not meet that requirement and a different architecture is needed (e.g., pre-binned aggregation with a daily cycle that explicitly resets state). At GridUnion's polling cadence (30 seconds), the post-hoc batch is fast enough.

Platform-level operational note: The platform-level monitoring rule that catches this class of issue is a 'state size growth rate' metric per streaming query. If state size grows at a positive rate over more than two watermark-interval periods (8 hours for a 4-hour watermark, 2 hours for a 1-hour watermark), the metric alerts and the on-call rotation reviews the grouping key composition. GridUnion's Platform team added this rule in 2026 Q1 after this exact incident.

Deferred short answer (Q5): Short answer (1–2 sentences). Suppose the team chose option C (move state to disk via RocksDB). What architectural risk would they be accepting that the option A separation does not have?
→ Disk-backed state still grows monotonically — RocksDB just delays the failure mode from 'driver OOM' to 'state checkpoint disk full' or 'recovery time exceeds operational tolerance'. Eventually the disk fills, or the recovery on restart takes hours because the state must be re-loaded from disk into memory. The architectural defect (unbounded state from the grouping key composition) is not fixed by changing the storage backend.

tasks.pssReference: PSS9 lesson 4 (State Management and Watermark Mechanics — eviction criterion), PSS9 lesson 7 (Streaming Antipatterns — derived-column grouping), PSS6 lesson 3 (Memory Management — driver state pressure).

tasks.title: Streaming Schema Evolution Mid-Stream

description.context: GridUnion Continental, the NL deployment's continuous meter-quality streaming pipeline. The pipeline has been running uninterrupted for 11 months. Yesterday afternoon the upstream NL Customer team's producer added an optional column 'meter_class' to the readings JSON payload, with a 30-day plan to migrate consumers. The streaming pipeline crashed within 3 minutes of the producer change with a schema mismatch error. The Customer team's runbook states that adding optional columns is backward-compatible; the Streaming team's runbook states that the streaming pipeline trusts whatever schema the source provides. Both runbooks are individually correct; the system is wrong.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — the team_context block names the operational pressures in play — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, expr, count, avg, when
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, TimestampType

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("nl_meter_quality_stream").getOrCreate()

# Schema declared at pipeline deployment 11 months ago
# Customer team's producer was emitting exactly these fields then;
# yesterday they added an optional meter_class field to the JSON payload.
schema = StructType([
    StructField("meter_id", StringType(), False),
    StructField("region", StringType(), False),
    StructField("daily_kw", DoubleType(), False),
    StructField("event_ts", TimestampType(), False)
])

readings = (spark.readStream
    .format("kafka")
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "kafka.gridunion.internal:9093")
    .option("subscribe", "meter_readings_nl")
    .load()
    .select(expr("CAST(value AS STRING)").alias("json"))
    .select(
        # from_json with strict mode (FAILFAST) — set 11 months ago by the
        # team that wrote this pipeline, who wanted any malformed reading
        # to surface as an error rather than a silent null
        expr("from_json(json, 'meter_id STRING, region STRING, daily_kw DOUBLE, event_ts TIMESTAMP', map('mode', 'FAILFAST'))").alias("r"))
    .select("r.*"))

# Per-region quality metrics, tumbling 5-minute window, 10-minute watermark
quality = (readings
    .withWatermark("event_ts", "10 minutes")
    .groupBy("region")
    .agg(
        count("*").alias("reading_count"),
        avg("daily_kw").alias("avg_kw"),
        count(when(col("daily_kw") < 0, 1)).alias("negative_count")
    ))

query = (quality.writeStream
    .format("delta")
    .outputMode("complete")
    .option("checkpointLocation", "/checkpoints/nl_meter_quality_stream/")
    .trigger(processingTime="30 seconds")
    .start("/silver_nl/meter_quality_5min/"))

query.awaitTermination()

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: The Customer team (producer side) operates under a 'add optional columns is non-breaking' policy that has been in their runbook for 4 years. They added meter_class as an optional field, communicated the change in their team channel, and proceeded. The Streaming team (consumer side) operates under a 'trust the source schema' policy and uses FAILFAST parsing to catch malformed inputs early. The two policies are individually defensible. There is no schema contract between the teams that names what 'optional column' means at the parser layer, what 'backward-compatible' means at the consumer layer, or what each team owes the other when a producer schema evolves. The streaming pipeline's 11-month uninterrupted run was a function of producer stability rather than of architectural resilience.

fields.label: Q1 — The Customer team added an OPTIONAL column. Why did the streaming pipeline crash on the very first message containing the new field?
options: The new column changed the JSON byte size of the message past the Kafka consumer's max.partition.fetch.bytes threshold; the consumer rejected the oversized message and the streaming query crashed when its retry limit was reached.

The from_json parse with FAILFAST mode rejects any field in the input JSON that is not declared in the schema; FAILFAST treats unknown fields as a parse error rather than as a non-breaking extra, even when the unknown field is genuinely optional from the producer's perspective.

The Spark streaming engine validates incoming Kafka messages against the checkpoint's recorded schema fingerprint; the new field changed the fingerprint and the resume operation failed because checkpoint-vs-current schema mismatch is fatal in append mode.

The Delta sink's schema enforcement detected the new column propagated through the streaming aggregation and rejected the write because 'meter_class' was not part of the destination table's declared schema.
fields.correctAnswer: The from_json parse with FAILFAST mode rejects any field in the input JSON that is not declared in the schema; FAILFAST treats unknown fields as a parse error rather than as a non-breaking extra, even when the unknown field is genuinely optional from the producer's perspective.
fields.rationale:
Spark's from_json function with mode='FAILFAST' requires the input JSON to match the declared schema exactly — any field present in the JSON that is not in the schema causes a parse error, which FAILFAST raises as an exception that crashes the stream. The producer team's notion of 'optional column' is a contract-layer concept: from their perspective, adding a field that consumers can ignore is non-breaking. From the parser's perspective, an unknown field is unknown — the parser has no way to know the producer considers it optional. FAILFAST is doing exactly what it was configured to do; the configuration is the gap. The 11 months of clean operation reflect 11 months without any producer-side schema evolution, not a property of the pipeline.

When this answer is wrong: On a non-strict parser configuration (PERMISSIVE or DROPMALFORMED), the failure mode is different — see Q2. The diagnosis here depends on the FAILFAST mode being explicitly visible in the snippet.

fields.label: Q2 — If the parser had been configured with mode 'PERMISSIVE' instead of FAILFAST, what would have happened?
options: PERMISSIVE mode silently drops fields not declared in the schema; the streaming pipeline would have continued running, ignoring the meter_class column entirely as if the producer had not added it. This is non-disruptive but means the consumer never sees the new field even after it intends to adopt it.

PERMISSIVE mode preserves unknown fields in a special _corrupt_record column and emits the well-formed columns alongside; the streaming pipeline would have continued running and the meter_class column would have appeared in the _corrupt_record column for later inspection.

PERMISSIVE mode triggers a one-time reschema detection that updates the running query's plan to include the new field; the streaming pipeline would have continued running and the meter_class column would have become a queryable column from that micro-batch onward.

PERMISSIVE mode logs each unknown field at the WARN level and continues parsing; the streaming pipeline would have continued running, the warning would have been visible in the driver log, and the field would have remained unprojected.
fields.correctAnswer: PERMISSIVE mode silently drops fields not declared in the schema; the streaming pipeline would have continued running, ignoring the meter_class column entirely as if the producer had not added it. This is non-disruptive but means the consumer never sees the new field even after it intends to adopt it.
fields.rationale:
PERMISSIVE mode silently drops fields not in the declared schema. The pipeline would not have crashed, but the meter_class column would have been invisible to the consumer — the consumer's view of the data is fixed at the schema declared 11 months ago. This is the silent counterpart of the FAILFAST failure: FAILFAST raises a loud error; PERMISSIVE produces a quiet drift between what the producer intends and what the consumer sees. Neither mode is correct without a schema contract; FAILFAST surfaces the gap loudly, PERMISSIVE hides it. Option B describes _corrupt_record handling which applies to malformed JSON, not unknown fields. Options C and D are fabricated mechanisms.

When this answer is wrong: If the parse mode were DROPMALFORMED, the behavior is similar to PERMISSIVE for unknown fields but ALSO drops rows that fail to parse; the diagnosis is operationally similar but slightly different at the row-loss boundary.

fields.label: Q3 — Identify the architectural fault — the absent artifact whose presence would have prevented this incident.
